issue_title: Query Args Not Encoding with %20 Instead of +
issue_body:
Hello,

Apologies if I am missing something obvious - I am a fit of a python/furl novice.

In the example below, what I would like to do is achieved:

>>> f.query = 'space=jams&woofs=squeeze+dog'
>>> f.query.encode()
'space=jams&woofs=squeeze+dog'
>>> f.query.encode(';')
'space=jams;woofs=squeeze+dog'
>>> f.query.encode(quote_plus=False)
'space=jams&woofs=squeeze%20dog'

However, trying to replace this exact example doesn't yield the same result.

For the following:
f.args ['X'] = [A B C 1 2 3]

What I would like to achieve is not have these spaces encoded as + but rather %20. However, I haven't been able to get that to work.
Sorry if I missed something obvious.

issue_title: Plim doesn't respect mako's ## comments properly.
issue_body:
I know that Plim has it's own comment feature ( / ) but ignoring Mako's commenting symbols ( ## ) means that you have to change the mako files to work with plim. Normally, that's no big deal, since plim supports its own versions of what mako does, and in your documents, you can just use plim's / But it means that we can't put comments in inherited or namespaced files, which may NOT be plim files, but are "pure" mako files. For example, I have a helpers.mako file that contains some things I want to pull into other documents/templates. So far, so good; I can grab these through standard Mako tools, either in pure mako or in plim. But if I want to put a comment in that PURE mako file, plim doesn't respect it. The below will output both sets of text if plim is used as a pre-processor on documents that inherit this block and use it. It seems to want to make a div with the id "#" which is non-sensical (and perhaps invalid html) but makes sense according to the #myid use case. ``` <%block name="something_to_use_elsewhere">

This should be here.

## But this should not. ``` renders as ``` <%block name="something_to_use_elsewhere">

This should be here.

But this should not.
``` And if I change the ## into a / to suit plim, then I have invalid mako, and other code can't use it. Well, not invalid, but again, it's not interpreted as a comment. What's weird is that in the docs here http://plim.readthedocs.org/en/latest/en/syntax.html#new-style-blocks it uses a # to make a comment in the generated mako code, but not at the beginning of a line. I don't see anyplace where ## is already used by plim, can we make it either pass through, or ... something? Losing `
` doesn't seem so bad ;-) tl;dr, can we have mako comments back?

issue_title: Encapsulate _init__ imports in try/catch (individually)
issue_body:
On Windows, it is an incredible pain to install sqlitebck. Because of that, I opted for installing gluish without dependencies, and just use the parts that I needed.

However, this cherry-picking approach does not work because you import all the sub-modules in the _init__.py - this makes import fail no matter what parts I actually want to use, because this means all the dependencies have to be present.

I have two alternative solutions:

Solution 1: Don't import in __init__.py like you do.
Solution 2: Make the imports conditional, enclose them in try/catch like here: http://stackoverflow.com/a/3496790/1319284. This means convenient imports if the dependencies are there, no convenient imports otherwise.

issue_title: Make pythonz-bd optional
issue_body:
Move this dependency to extras_require, and show an error message to the user if he tries to install a new python version, but pythonz is not installed. This change would make the linux packages more user-friendly. 

e.g. for the archlinux package, since the package is not in the official repositories, when you try to generate the package, you get an error because the package depends on pythonz-bd. So you need to install pythonz-bd before, but then you get a new error, because pythonz-db depends on resumable-urlretrieve. The main problem  with the linux packages, is that for every dependency you add on your python package, you need to generate a new linux package, so it would be easier for the packagers to keep the mandatory dependencies to the minimum.

issue_title: Clarification on Fixing the Value of Parameter
issue_body:
For the following example, I was hoping to get some clarification on the parameter c.  My reading of this syntax is that the value of c should not change through the fitting routine.  However, upon examination of the fit results it appears as though it has, in fact, be altered.  Is there a way to truly fix the value so it doesn't change or am I simply misunderstanding the approach? Currently, I'm using symfit 0.4.0 and python 3.

```
from symfit import parameters, variables, exp, Fit, Parameter

a, b, c, d = parameters('a, b, c, d')
x, y = variables('x, y')

c.value = 4.0
c.fixed = True

model_dict = {y: a * exp(-(x - b)**2 / (2 * c**2)) + d}
fit = Fit(model_dict, x=xdata, y=ydata)
fit_result = fit.execute()
```
The output of the fit_result.params call is and I would expect c to still be 4.0:

```
OrderedDict([('a', 16.374368575343127),
             ('b', 0.49201249437123556),
             ('c', 0.5337962977235504),
             ('d', -9.55593614465743)])
```

Cheers,
Brian

issue_title: libpng support for macOS Sierra (10.12.5)
issue_body:
I use the latest repo version of the dlib and stated in the title OS. It can be spotted that some users have encountered this issue too (using some small internet search).

On some systems when we try to load PNG images using a `load_image` function, an exception with a comment 

> png_loader: parse error in file some_file.png

 is thrown. If we debug this function, we'll be able to see that an error occurs (created `png_structp` structure is `NULL`) on the line:

`ld_->png_ptr_ = png_create_read_struct( PNG_LIBPNG_VER_STRING, NULL, &png_loader_user_error_fn_silent, &png_loader_user_warning_fn_silent );`

of "dlib/dlib/image_loader/png_loader.cpp" file. 

I could fix this by manually replacing `PNG_LIBPNG_VER_STRING` with a version string of the latest libpng installed in my system via Homebrew:

`ld_->png_ptr_ = png_create_read_struct( "1.6.30", NULL, &png_loader_user_error_fn_silent, &png_loader_user_warning_fn_silent );`

Now PNG images' loading is correct, but I'm afraid that this solution is not quite clear. I'll try to make a better solution, please let me know, if a better way of such solution is already available.

On Windows 10 with Nvidia videocard you need exactly this:

issue_title: Tree structure and _paper_trade
issue_body:
Hi Phil,

could you please explain how does (and should) tree structure actually work? Do I understand correctly that the actual money is allocated (fees are paid and `_capital` is changed) only at the `Rebalance()` of the parent? If so - why do we change weights of securities and prices of the parent strategy?

For example here's an example of quite unexpected behavior. Below is the same strategy which is backtested stand-alone and as a part of another strategy:
```
data = bt.get('spy,eem', start='2014-01-01')

com = lambda q, p: 10 * abs(q)

mom_s = bt.Strategy('mom_s', [bt.algos.RunMonthly(),
                              bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                              bt.algos.SelectMomentum(1),
                              bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                              bt.algos.Rebalance()],
                    ['spy', 'eem'])

from copy import deepcopy
mom_s1 = deepcopy(mom_s)

master = bt.Strategy('master', [bt.algos.RunMonthly(),
                                bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                                bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                                bt.algos.Rebalance()],
                     [mom_s])

t0 = bt.Backtest(mom_s1, data, commissions=com)
r0 = bt.run(t0)

t = bt.Backtest(master, data, commissions=com)
r = bt.run(t)
```
Note I chose quite a substantial commission to make an effect vivid. Also I have here `deepcopy` explicitly, so you can avoid #19 and test it independently from merging #20.

The performance of `master` and `mom_s` differ quite dramatically:
```
ax = r.prices.plot()
r0.prices.plot(color='r', ax=ax)
```
And the reason is commissions that are paid in `mom_s` and ignored in `master`.

So far I don't understand the whole "paper trading" in the `bt.core`, so I don't see how to fix this issue easily. Could you please advise?

issue_title: Picket Fence: picket spacing may not be accurate in some cases
issue_body: In some special cases the picket fence algorithm may miscalculate the spacing between detected pickets. This applies when a "num_pickets" argument is given to the picket fence "analyze" method. When the pickets are extracted from the profile with a max number of pickets given, the return order is not guaranteed to be sorted. If not, the spacing may be 0 or <0.

issue_title: Using `vcrpy` as a dynamic callback on `HTTPretty`
issue_body:
In Node.js, I have written and used 2 libraries for HTTP mocking/playback: [`fixed-server`][] and [`eight-track`][] (currently actually [`spy-server`][]/[`nine-track`][]). The gist is `fixed-server` handles routing to determine whether to use a static fixture (e.g. something from disk) or to use a playback utility (e.g. `eight-track`). `eight-track` is similar to `vcrpy` but it doesn't patch underlying libraries -- it only accepts request objects.

More info can be found here: http://twolfson.com/2015-01-11-testing-with-other-services

We want to bring this functionality over to Python. Currently, we are using [`HTTPretty`][] for static and pseudo-dynamic responses but nothing with automatic playback like `vcrpy`. Since `HTTPretty` has routing built in and supports dynamic responses, we wanted to use it as the "router" (i.e. `fixed-server`). This means `vcrpy` would become an optional dynamic callback.

https://github.com/gabrielfalcao/HTTPretty/tree/0.8.3#dynamic-responses-through-callbacks

We tried it out and after a bit of fidgeting, we got a wrapper function that can take parameters from `HTTPretty`, make the request via `vcrpy`, and return the response information to `HTTPretty`.

```python
# Load in our dependencies
import urlparse
import httpretty
from vcr.cassette import Cassette
from vcr.stubs import VCRHTTPConnection, VCRHTTPSConnection

# Create our cassette
cassette = Cassette.load(path='cassette.yaml')


# Define our vcrpy-backed HTTPretty callback
def vcr_request_callback(request, url, httpretty_res_headers):
    # Interpret our URL and select the connection class
    # https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/v.1.7.4/vcr/stubs/__init__.py#L340-L350
    url_parts = urlparse.urlparse(url)
    if url_parts.scheme == 'http':
        VCRProtocolConnection = VCRHTTPConnection
    elif url_parts.scheme == 'https':
        VCRProtocolConnection = VCRHTTPSConnection
    else:
        raise RuntimeError('Unrecognized URL scheme "{scheme}" from "{url}"'
                           .format(scheme=url_parts.scheme, url=url))

    # Generate an HTTP/HTTPS connection using the `netloc` (accepted in Python 2)
    # https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/v.1.7.4/vcr/stubs/__init__.py#L307-L316
    # https://docs.python.org/2/library/httplib.html#httplib.HTTPConnection
    conn = VCRProtocolConnection(url_parts.netloc)
    conn.cassette = cassette

    # Populate our request
    # https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/v.1.7.4/vcr/stubs/__init__.py#L161-L174
    conn.request(method=request.method, url=url, body=request.body, headers=request.headers)

    # Collect our response
    # https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/v.1.7.4/vcr/stubs/__init__.py#L215-L271
    # DEV: We need to disable HTTPretty otherwise we encounter recursive requests
    # TODO: Research if this has implications for requests in a threaded application
    httpretty.disable()
    res = conn.getresponse()
    httpretty.enable()

    # Save updated cassettte
    # https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/v.1.7.4/vcr/cassette.py#L270-L277
    cassette._save()

    # Return our response information to HTTPretty
    return (res.status, dict(res.headers), res.read())

# Register an HTTPretty endpoint to use vcr_request_callback
httpretty.register_uri(httpretty.GET, 'http://google.com/hello', body=vcr_request_callback)

# Register another endpoint to use a static response
httpretty.register_uri(httpretty.GET, 'http://google.com/world', body='world')
```

Now that the proof of concept is complete, we would love to add it into `vcrpy` or maybe build a wrapper library that combine the two. Before doing that though, we wanted to get your feedback and thoughts.

**Side notes:** We are actually using [`httpretty-fixtures`][] in Python to make our fixtures more reusable across tests.

[`fixed-server`]: https://github.com/uber/fixed-server
[`eight-track`]: https://github.com/uber/eight-track
[`spy-server`]: https://github.com/underdogio/spy-server
[`nine-track`]: https://github.com/twolfson/nine-track
[`HTTPretty`]: https://github.com/gabrielfalcao/HTTPretty
[`httpretty-fixtures`]: https://github.com/underdogio/httpretty-fixtures

issue_title: Problem with send_command() for Cisco context-sensitive prompts
issue_body:
By default, send_command() auto-finds the current prompt and uses this to match the prompt returned by the command.  However, some Cisco commands change the prompt according to context.  Here's a simple example from the SSH console of the Nexus 9000 APIC I'm using:

apic1# conf t
apic1(config)# dns
apic1(config-dns)# show clock
Time : 06:35:08.450 UTC Thu Nov 30 2017

apic1(config-dns)# exit
apic1(config)# end
apic1#

When I run send_command('dns') as part of a Netmiko script, it times out, as it's looking for the prompt 'apic1(config)#', which it doesn't find as the actual prompt returned is 'apic1(config-dns)#'.

To keep my script simple, I would like the default prompt in config mode to match the regular expression, 'apic1\(config.*\)', so I can iterate a list of commands like this:

config_commands = [
    'dns',
    '  show clock',
    '  exit'
    ]

without having to worry about setting the prompt for each command.

This works:

config_commands = [
    'dns ; show clock ; exit',
    ]

but doesn't match the format of the Cisco show running-config output.

Am I missing something obvious?  Can you suggest an alternative to my workaround, please?

issue_title: Improve Test Suite for Other Users
issue_body:
Currently, tests require a module that's not part of the repository (for security reasons).  However, there's no documentation (other than a note in an issue) as to how to supply the necessary information.  Additionally, the tests have hard-set expectations for prompts (and possibly other items).  See excerpt below.

```python
    module.EXPECTED_RESPONSES = {
        'base_prompt'      : 'pynet-rtr1',
        'user_exec_prompt' : 'pynet-rtr1>',
        'enable_prompt'    : 'pynet-rtr1#',
        'interface_ip'     : '10.220.88.20',
        'config_mode'      : '(config)',
    }
```

This means that if a user contributing code (and new tests) wants to actually test his/her changes, he/she must have a device setup precisely this way.  Being able to parse a YAML file (in `.gitignore`) with credentials, expected prompts, IPs, etc. would significantly increase the flexibility and accessibility of these tests.

issue_title: sonify.chord, X should be silent but sounds like garbage
issue_body:
Title says it all.

Background: the chord module distinguishes two "non-chord" labels:

- `N` (no-chord) used to indicate things like silence, crowd noise, or sounds that have no harmony
- `X` (unknown) used to indicate, as far as I can tell, out-of-gamut chords.

The `X` label mainly comes up when a predictor can only operate in a reduced label space (eg, is not allowed to predict 9ths), but you still want to model those observations as distinct from `N`.

When these are encoded via [`chord.encode`](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/chord.py#L99-L102), the `N` label maps to an empty set of pitch classes.  The `X` label maps to a vector of all `-1`, which gets sonified as button-mashing the keyboard.

Proposed change: `sonify.time_frequency` thresholds `gram` at 0 so that negative amplitudes are treated as silence.  This would solve the problem indirectly, and prevent similar mishaps from popping up elsewhere.

issue_title: offsetof() support is incomplete
issue_body:
pycparser 2.13 added support for offsetof, and it is listed in the CHANGES file as "offsetof() the way gcc implements it". However, there are two differences between gcc's implemenation and pycparser's.

The important one: pycparser defines the syntax for `offsetof()` as 
```
        primary_expression  : OFFSETOF LPAREN type_name COMMA identifier RPAREN
```
but [gcc defines it](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc-4.9.1/gcc/Offsetof.html) as 
```
     primary:
             "__builtin_offsetof" "(" typename "," offsetof_member_designator ")"
     
     offsetof_member_designator:
               identifier
             | offsetof_member_designator "." identifier
             | offsetof_member_designator "[" expr "]"

```

The less important one (also visible above) is that gcc supports spelling `offsetof` as `__builtin_offsetof`.

issue_title: Throwing error when using invalid topic name
issue_body:
The library does not throw any exception or error when using a topic name that is not valid.
The only legal characters in a topic are:
`a-z`, `A-Z`, `0-9`,`.`, `_` and `-`
See: https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/0.9.0/core/src/main/scala/kafka/common/Topic.scala#L25
This character constraint exists due to [this](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-495).

Maybe it would be a good idea to inform the library user that the topic is not valid. I just spent an hour of debugging because a topic contained a `/`. Therefore, the connection timed out with a `Failed to update metadata after %s secs.` error which does not seem to be related on first sight.

issue_title: Bug in interpreter of DW_CFA_remember_state
issue_body:
Dear all

I believe there is a small bug in the interpreter of DW_CFA_remember_state.  The interpreter pushes the current line on a stack, but, due to sharing, subsequent bytecode instructions can modify the pushed line whenever they modify the current line.

For instance, this FDE entry:

00000088 000000000000001c 0000005c FDE cie=00000030 pc= 0000000000400980.. 00000000004009d1
  DW_CFA_advance_loc: 4 to 0000000000400984
  DW_CFA_def_cfa_offset: 24
  DW_CFA_advance_loc1: 64 to 00000000004009c4
  DW_CFA_remember_state
  DW_CFA_def_cfa_offset: 8
  DW_CFA_advance_loc: 4 to 00000000004009c8
  DW_CFA_restore_state

should generate:

00000060 000000000000001c 00000034 FDE cie=00000030 pc=0000000000400980..00000000004009d1
   LOC           CFA      ra
0000000000400980 rsp+8    c-8
0000000000400984 rsp+24   c-8
00000000004009c4 rsp+8    c-8
00000000004009c8 rsp+24   c-8

 (observe the last +24, due to remembering the line for 400984), while it results in:

00000060 000000000000001c 0000000000000034 FDE cie=00000030 pc=0000000000400980..00000000004009d1
   LOC   CFA      ra
0000000000400980 rsp+8    c-8
0000000000400984 rsp+24   c-8
00000000004009c4 rsp+8    c-8
00000000004009c8 rsp+8    c-8

The final +8 is due due the update to the current line in 4009c4.  

I believe that deep-copying the current line before pushing it on the stack + updating the pc when pulling is a simple fix (patch below).

I cannot submit you a binary to reproduce easily as the FDE entry was obtained by parsing eh_frame info via an eh_frame parser I implemented (that I will eventually contribute to pyelftools) and not by parsing debug_frames.

-francesco

```
Index: pyelftools/elftools/dwarf/callframe.py
===================================================================
--- pyelftools/elftools/dwarf/callframe.py  (original)
+++ pyelftools/elftools/dwarf/callframe.py  (working copy)
@@ -501,9 +501,11 @@
                 else:
                     cur_line.pop(instr.args[0], None)
             elif name == 'DW_CFA_remember_state':
-                line_stack.append(cur_line)
+                line_stack.append(copy.deepcopy(cur_line))
             elif name == 'DW_CFA_restore_state':
+                pc = cur_line['pc'] 
                 cur_line = line_stack.pop()
+                cur_line['pc'] = pc

         # The current line is appended to the table after all instructions
         # have ended, in any case (even if there were no instructions).
```

issue_title: Investigate screen refresh speed
issue_body:
After merging https://github.com/michael-lazar/rtv/pull/431, I've noticed that the using the cursor to scroll through submissions is noticeably slower than it used to be. This is unfortunate but understandable because in order to generalize the text colors and attributes, I had to add a layer of indirection every time text is written to the screen.

Before releasing the next version, I would like to profile the ``page.draw()`` method and see if there are any easy performance gains that can be made.

issue_title: Query with Application Resource Locks Terminated during Processing
issue_body:
### Environment

- Python: 3.6.1 (32-bit), 3.4.3
- pyodbc: 4.0.17, 4.0.16
- OS: Windows, Linux
- DB: SQL Server 2012, SQL Server 2016
- driver: ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server, ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server, SQL Server Native Client 11.0

### Issue

I have a stored procedure that takes in a list of identifiers and then deletes those identifiers' data from a series of about thirty tables. In my context, it's very possible that multiple copies of this stored procedure could be running concurrently (working on different identifiers), or that other stored procedures writing to those same thirty tables will be running at the same time (again, with different identifiers). In order to avoid deadlocks from table-level locks taken out by the delete statements and the other procedures trying to run, I wrapped every delete statement in this stored procedure in an application resource lock. It looks something like the below.

```SQL
CREATE PROCEDURE [dbo].[deleteData] @IDs dbo.IDTableType READONLY
AS
BEGIN

DECLARE @result INT;

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable1', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable1 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable1', @LockOwner = 'Session';

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable2', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable2 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable2', @LockOwner = 'Session';

--... (etc) ...

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable30', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable30 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable30', @LockOwner = 'Session';
END
```
I execute the stored procedure through something like the following python script:

```python
import pyodbc

connection_string = 'Driver=ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server;ApplicationIntent=ReadWrite;MultiSubnetFailover=No;' \
                            'Trusted_Connection=yes;Server=localhost;Database=mydb'
db_handle = pyodbc.connect(connection_string, autocommit=True)

query = 'DECLARE @IDs [dbo].IDTableType; ' \
          'INSERT INTO @IDs (ID) VALUES (1) ' \
          '{call [mydb].[dbo].deleteData(@IDs)};'
print('SQL DELETE QUERY:\n%s' % (query))
db_handle.execute(query)
```

When I run the following statement through pyodbc, the stored procedure winds up getting terminated partway through processing. So, I'll wind up with a number of the tables having their data cleared out and a number not. The number not cleared out isn't always the same, but it's never zero, so re-running the query numerous times never achieves the intended result. Perhaps worth noting, is that pyodbc doesn't fail or anything; the SQL statement's execution is aborted, but my python program continues to run fine (perhaps because I'm not capturing and examining some output...).

Intriguingly, if I remove the application resource lock blocks from around the delete statements in the stored procedure, pyodbc runs it to completion, as I expect. Also, just running the procedure (with the applock blocks) directly in SQL Server Management Studio works as I'd expect, as does running the stored procedure through other connections, like Perl's DBI module. I'm mostly curious as to whether I'm doing something wrong in my use of pyodbc, or whether this might actually be some bug. I've had a hard time finding relevant-looking problems and documentation in searches over the past couple days.

issue_title: selinux module install check refers to binary not in user path
issue_body:
The selinux module tries to check whether selinux is installed.

`if not self.py3.check_commands(['getenforce']):`

this effectively runs `which getenforce`. The getenforce binary is installed under /usr/sbin (gentoo, also fedora as far as i read) which is not in the regular user path and therefore not found, resulting in the message `selinux: isn't installed`.
Removing the check makes the module work as expected.

issue_title: Publishing linux egg files is problematic
issue_body:
Since version 1.1.8 a generic linux binary egg has been distributed through pypi. I'm guessing this is to help users that won't or can't install a compiler. Unfortunately this causes problems for users of easy-install that don't have the same "python 2.7" used to build the egg.

For example, when installing pykerberos for pypy easy_install grabs the egg file:

```
$ ./bin/pypy -m easy_install pykerberos
Searching for pykerberos
Reading https://pypi.python.org/simple/pykerberos/
Best match: pykerberos 1.1.9
Downloading https://pypi.python.org/packages/2.7/p/pykerberos/pykerberos-1.1.9-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg#md5=4e6672337f0420c6964de0319566decf
Processing pykerberos-1.1.9-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg
...
```

The installation completes without issue, but the interpreter faults when importing kerberos:

```
./bin/pypy
Python 2.7.10 (f3ad1e1e1d6215e20d34bb65ab85ff9188c9f559, Sep 01 2015, 11:00:11)
[PyPy 2.6.1 with GCC 4.9.2] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>>> import kerberos
Fatal Python error: PyThreadState_Get: no current thread
Aborted
```
Reinstalling with pip avoids the problem since pip does not support egg packages and builds the project from source:

```
./bin/pypy -m pip install pykerberos
Collecting pykerberos
  Downloading pykerberos-1.1.9.tar.gz
Building wheels for collected packages: pykerberos
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for pykerberos
...

$ ./bin/pypy 
Python 2.7.10 (f3ad1e1e1d6215e20d34bb65ab85ff9188c9f559, Sep 01 2015, 11:00:11)
[PyPy 2.6.1 with GCC 4.9.2] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>>> import kerberos
```

There is nothing you can do to solve this problem in pykerberos. I would suggest removing the egg packages from pypi to avoid problems for future users. Also, note that pypi disallows uploading generic wheel binaries to avoid this exact problem. See the note here:

https://python-packaging-user-guide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/distributing/#platform-wheels

issue_title: Option to color nodes based on time spent in function rather than whole subtree
issue_body:
This is a feature request to add a flag to color graph nodes based on time spent in each function, rather than coloring based on time spend in _the whole subtree_.

Concrete example (from https://github.com/jrfonseca/gprof2dot/blob/master/sample.png): `main` is colored red (since it's the call tree root), yet it's likely not a good place to look for optimizations. Same goes with small functions on top of expensive calls (`read_all_makefiles`). They should probably not be colored "hot" if they're fast. The expensive function call should!

I will look into implementing this soon if I have some spare time.

issue_title: Speedtest.net configuration fetching is broken on v0.3.2 and Python 2.7.9
issue_body:
```
$ speedtest --version
0.3.2
$ speedtest
Retrieving speedtest.net configuration...
Could not retrieve speedtest.net configuration
$ python --version
Python 2.7.9
```

Apparently certificate validation has been enabled by default in [Python 2.7.9](http://lwn.net/Articles/611243/), and this breaks the fetching of the speedtest.net configuration.

If certificate validation is disabled by applying the following quick and dirty fix, the program works as expected:

```
194,195d193
<     import ssl
<
197,200c195
<         ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
<         ctx.check_hostname = False
<         ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
<         uh = urlopen(request, context = ctx)
---
>         uh = urlopen(request)
```

issue_title: Encode MP3 at 320kb/s instead of 128 kb/s
issue_body: When you download a track it is always downloaded as 128kb/s (one of the lowest average quality bit rates for an MP3 file). There should be functionality to support at least trying to download a track at 320kb/s before resorting to 128kb/s. I've been looking into it, but not sure which module it is dependent on.

issue_title: Records silently ignores duplicate field name in joined tables.
issue_body:
In an SQL query I had joined to tables that happened to have a field with the same name (with different content). Records selected one of the fields without complaining; it just happened to be the wrong one. 

```sql
SELECT *
FROM A LEFT OUTER JOIN B on A.somekey=B.somekey
WHERE .....
```
If A and B contain a field with the same name, just one is returned. Usually the table name is prefixed to distinguish them, but I assume Records just uses the fieldname without prefix. That is usually convenient but not in this case. I think I'd prefer an error message.

issue_title: Breathe doesn't support external documentation (eg: tagfiles)
issue_body:
Here's an example XML output ``` (xAH) Lord Stark:~/xAODAnaHelpers/docs (uncrustify)$ ack pt_sort doxygen/xml/ doxygen/xml/_helper_functions_8h.xml 845: HelperFunctions::pt_sort 1205:structpt_sort 1234:std::sort(sortedCont.begin(),sortedCont.end(),pt_sort()); 1243:std::sort(sortedCont.begin(),sortedCont.end(),pt_sort()); ``` Doxygen's generated HTML gives ``` operator() (const xAOD::IParticle &lhs, const xAOD::IParticle &rhs) ``` Note that my `Doxyfile` has `TAGFILES = "tagfiles/rootcore.tag=https://ucatlas.github.io/RootCoreDocumentation/2.4.28/"` in it. Meanwhile, the generated HTML from Breathe+Sphinx has ```
bool operator()(const xAOD::IParticle &lhs, const xAOD::IParticle &rhs)¶
``` Any idea how to get this to have the TAGFILE linked correctly?

issue_title: Slow board tested with line_profiler under 100 games of 10e6
issue_body:
[slow_performance_board_fen_scan.txt](https://github.com/niklasf/python-chess/files/1507212/slow_performance_board_fen_scan.txt)
I tested chess.pgn and found performance problems.  First seek position is slow - I replaced it and speed up 10x times at least I am want to process https://database.lichess.org/.

Second I processed some game and want analyze them but still code is slow since this.

Let it be my testing code:

```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    profile_lines = True
    if profile_lines:
        profiler = LineProfiler()
        profiler.add_module(sys.modules[__name__])
        profiler.add_module(sys.modules['chess.pgn'])
        profiler.add_module(sys.modules['chess'])
        profiler.enable_by_count()

issue_title: Handle format translations in format replace
issue_body:
Regex, when doing format replacements, allows for `!s`, `!r`, and `!a` (though `!a` is omitted in PY2).

For completeness, add support for these even if it is unlikely that these would ever be used.  There isn't really a practical case for this, it would just be nice if these don't break things.

Regex actually applies these to the capture group except when specifying a sub capture `{1[2]}`.  This is weird and I don't care to mimic that, so this will apply to the captured string.

Due to the complexity of handling string casing, we mimic formatting without actually calling the "format" function on the template's string object.  For this reason, formatting actually works in byte strings because our algorithm has no restriction in this case. Even though `{}` style formatting is not officially supported byte strings.

We will most likely implement things like they are in with Python 3.5's `%` style byte string formatting:

- `!r` == `repr(bstring).encode('ascii','backslashreplace')`
- `!a` will work like `!r`
- `!s` probably shouldn't actually be supported as `!b` would actually make more sense, but for now, we'll make this work like this: `bstring.encode('ascii','backslashreplace')` and keep things consistent and not add something new like `!b`.

For Unicode strings, we'll just do the expected things:

- `!s` == `str(string)`
- `!a` == `ascii(string)`
- `!r` == `repr(string)`

issue_title: Use the _pb2 suffix in the file name for protocol files generated with protoc
issue_body:
Hi,

since python-protobuf 3.0.0 landed in Debian, programs using `python-axolotl` fail with a message like this:

```
$ gajim
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "gajim.py", line 533, in <module>
    interface.run()
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/gui_interface.py", line 2733, in run
    gajim.plugin_manager = plugins.PluginManager()
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/plugins/helpers.py", line 129, in __call__
    cls.instance=super(Singleton, cls).__call__(*args,**kw)
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/plugins/pluginmanager.py", line 104, in __init__
    pc = PluginManager.scan_dir_for_plugins(path)
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/plugins/helpers.py", line 114, in wrapper
    result = f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/plugins/pluginmanager.py", line 454, in scan_dir_for_plugins
    module = __import__(module_name)
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/common/demandimport.py", line 95, in _demandimport
    return _import(name, globals, locals, fromlist, level)
  File "/home/ao2/.local/share/gajim/plugins/omemo/__init__.py", line 50, in <module>
    from omemo.state import OmemoState
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/common/demandimport.py", line 114, in _demandimport
    mod = _origimport(name, globals, locals)
  File "/home/ao2/.local/share/gajim/plugins/omemo/omemo/state.py", line 29, in <module>
    from axolotl.protocol.prekeywhispermessage import PreKeyWhisperMessage
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/common/demandimport.py", line 114, in _demandimport
    mod = _origimport(name, globals, locals)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/axolotl/protocol/prekeywhispermessage.py", line 5, in <module>
    from .whispermessage import WhisperMessage
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/common/demandimport.py", line 112, in _demandimport
    return _origimport(name, globals, locals, fromlist, level)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/axolotl/protocol/whispermessage.py", line 4, in <module>
    from . import whisperprotos
  File "/usr/share/gajim/src/common/demandimport.py", line 112, in _demandimport
    return _origimport(name, globals, locals, fromlist, level)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/axolotl/protocol/whisperprotos.py", line 41, in <module>
    options=None),
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/google/protobuf/descriptor.py", line 501, in __new__
    _message.Message._CheckCalledFromGeneratedFile()
TypeError: Descriptors should not be created directly, but only retrieved from their parent.
```

The same happens when running the tests with `python2.7 -m unittest discover -v`.

As analyzed in Debian bug #835271 (https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=835271) it looks like this is an issue in `python-axolotl` rather than in `python-protobuf` itself, basically the newer protobuf code checks that the generated protocol files have the `_pb2.py` suffix in their names, and this does not happen in `python-axolotl`.

The issue happens only when PyPy is not used (https://github.com/google/protobuf/blob/2fe0556c7abbe31c02147b9171397737a35bfe3b/python/google/protobuf/pyext/descriptor.cc#L94) which apparently is the case for the `python-protobuf` Debian package.

This behavior also looks consistent with the protbuf documentation: https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/pythontutorial#compiling-your-protocol-buffers even if the doc does not state clearly that having the `_pb2` suffix is a requirement.

Anyway, renaming the generated protbuf files fixes the issue, see this patch:
https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?att=1;bug=835271;filename=0001-Keep-the-_pb2-suffix-in-the-name-for-protobuf-genera.patch;msg=22

Should I submit a pull request? Against the develop branch?

Another question: the protobuf documentation suggests to use the same base name used for the .proto files also in the generated files, in `python-axolotl` that would mean having `WhisperTextProtocol_pb2.py` instead of `whisperprotos.py` and `LocalStorageProtocol_pb2.py` instead of `storageprotos.py`; or alternatively have the protocol files named `whisperprotos.proto` and `storageprotos.proto`.

Do you want to follow this scheme that makes it clear what the generated files are, or do you want to keep the current names?

Thanks,
   Antonio

issue_title: Next URLs shouldn't rely on session
issue_body:
Currently, the callback URIs are stored in the session. This works fine, except when you don't have reliable sessions (for example, a proxied API; different domains mean that the session will be different). If the callback URI were passed as part of `redirect_uri`, then it makes the request flow significantly more stateless.

In the case with a flakey session, this means that `redirect_uri` never gets passed to the provider, and we get HTTP !{200, 201}.

Also note that this results in a weird failure in `flask-oauthlib` too, where `authorized_response` *returns* an `OAuthException` rather than raises it (which is beside the point for the moment, but potentially a bug?)

**Relevant lines:**
Set the callback: https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/blob/8d4faabf17f2fa99ab5f927d3f94fbb1641ab914/flask_oauthlib/client.py#L522
Use the callback: https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/blob/8d4faabf17f2fa99ab5f927d3f94fbb1641ab914/flask_oauthlib/client.py#L626

issue_title: map is greedy
issue_body:
```
map includes this line:

  fs = [self.submit(fn, *args) for args in zip(*iterables)]

That means no futures code gets executed until all the iterables have been 
completely consumed.  If each iteration of an iterable can take quite some 
time, that means a long wait before any futures even starts.

It also means a humungous list is built up if the iterables returns many items. 
 For example if iterable was xrange(2000000) then that fs list is going to be 2 
million items long even if num_workers was something like 8.

A generator expression should be used instead.
```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `rogerbinns` on 7 Jun 2013 at 2:01

issue_title: Strange error only when reading URLs from file
issue_body:
Hey, I've just been experimenting a bit with requests-html, works as described so far, but I've encountered a strange issue that does not really seem to make any sense to me.

Before I go on, I have basically zero experience with Python, so please bear that in mind.. 😅

Best to describe my use case I was testing: Scraping info from Instagram, one of those 'modern' web sites ("web app") that serve and render all content via JavaScript, making my old school parsing scripts pretty useless, but that's what `html.render()` is for, I suppose..

So, the thing is, that code here is working as expected:
`test_a.py`
``` Python
from requests_html import HTMLSession
session = HTMLSession()

output_csv = 'URL' + ';' + 'Account Name' + ';' + 'Displayed Full Name' + ';' + 'Submitted Posts' + ';' + 'Followers'

sel_handle = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > div:nth-of-type(1) > h1'
sel_iposts = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > ul > li:nth-child(1) > span'
sel_follow = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > ul > li:nth-child(2) > span'
sel_dsname = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > div:nth-of-type(2) > h1'

URLSET = [
	"https://www.instagram.com/taylorswift/",
	"https://www.instagram.com/justinbieber/",
	"https://www.instagram.com/adele/",
	"https://www.instagram.com/onedirection/",
]

print(output_csv)

for entry in URLSET:
	r = session.get(entry)
	r.html.render()
	handle = r.html.find(sel_handle, first=True).text
	iposts = r.html.find(sel_iposts, first=True).text
	follow = r.html.find(sel_follow, first=True).text
	dsname = r.html.find(sel_dsname, first=True).text
	output = entry + ';' + handle + ';' + dsname + ';' + iposts + ';' + follow
	print(output)

```

But this code here **does not work**, although it should do the exact same thing:
`test_b.py`
``` Python
from requests_html import HTMLSession
session = HTMLSession()

output_csv = 'URL' + ';' + 'Account Name' + ';' + 'Displayed Full Name' + ';' + 'Submitted Posts' + ';' + 'Followers'

sel_handle = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > div:nth-of-type(1) > h1'
sel_iposts = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > ul > li:nth-child(1) > span'
sel_follow = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > ul > li:nth-child(2) > span'
sel_dsname = '#react-root > section > main > div > header > section > div:nth-of-type(2) > h1'

try:
	url_set = open('example_accs.txt')
except OSError:
	raise SystemExit('[Error] Could not open file "example_accs.txt" ...')

print(output_csv)

for entry in url_set:
	r = session.get(entry)
	r.html.render()
	handle = r.html.find(sel_handle, first=True).text
	iposts = r.html.find(sel_iposts, first=True).text
	follow = r.html.find(sel_follow, first=True).text
	dsname = r.html.find(sel_dsname, first=True).text
	output = entry + ';' + handle + ';' + dsname + ';' + iposts + ';' + follow
	print(output)

```

The only difference is that in `test_a.py` I use URLs that are stored in a list inside the source code, while in `test_b.py` I load those exact same URLs from an external text file.

For completeness, here is the content of `example_accs.txt`
```
https://www.instagram.com/taylorswift/
https://www.instagram.com/justinbieber/
https://www.instagram.com/adele/
https://www.instagram.com/onedirection/
```
Here is the error I get with `test_b.py`:
```
PS D:\Test> python test_b.py
URL;Account Name;Displayed Full Name;Submitted Posts;Followers
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_b.py", line 21, in <module>
    handle = r.html.find(sel_handle, first=True).text
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'text'
```
I understand that, `html.find` returns `None`, because the selector did not find anything.
But why is this only happening in `test_b.py`?

I tried to debug a bit around, of course, and the only relevant thing I've found so far is this, I think:

Fiddling with some `print(r.html)` inside the loops, I saw this:
```
PS D:\Test> python .\test_a.py
URL;Account Name;Displayed Full Name;Submitted Posts;Followers
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/taylorswift/'>
https://www.instagram.com/taylorswift/;taylorswift;Taylor Swift;144 posts;108m followers
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/justinbieber/'>
https://www.instagram.com/justinbieber/;justinbieber;Justin Bieber;4,345 posts;99.7m followers
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/adele/'>
https://www.instagram.com/adele/;adele;Adele;339 posts;32.8m followers
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/onedirection/'>
https://www.instagram.com/onedirection/;onedirection;One Direction;726 posts;17.1m followers
```
**vs.**
```
PS D:\Test> python test_b.py
URL;Account Name;Displayed Full Name;Submitted Posts;Followers
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/taylorswift/%0A'>
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/justinbieber/%0A'>
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/adele/%0A'>
<HTML url='https://www.instagram.com/onedirection/%0A'>
```

There is a difference in the `url` attribute (%0A), I don't know where that comes from..
Looks like some line ending shenanigans, but If I do this:
```python
for entry in url_set:
	print(entry)
```
Everything looks normal...
(I found out that Python automatically adds a `\n` when using `print`, but this isn't the issue itself, e.g.)
```python
for entry in url_set:
	print(entry, end='')
```
... looks just as expected.

Okay, just the usual background info. Please let me know if any relevant info is missing:
```
Python: 3.6.5 x64
OS: Windows 10 x64 v1803
Packages: All latest via pip
```

issue_title: TRACKABLE info is not stored when using login_user directly
issue_body:
We use
```
from flask_security import login_user
@app.route('/custom_login', methods=['POST'])
def custom_login():
    login_user(some_user)
    return redirect(...)
```
and noticed that information regarding current_login, last login, etc is not stored into the DB (sqlalchemy for us) unless we add a `db.session.commit()` after the `login_user(...)` call. 

After reading a bit the code, it looks like it could be fixed by adding a call to `after_this_request(db.session.commit)` in the `flask_securtity.utils.login_user` method. Not sure where we should add a unit test for this. Please if @mattupstate can hint us a bit on where is a good place to write the test for it, it would be great help to submit a PR and save you some work.

Thanks a lot.

issue_title: Inconsistent error handling
issue_body:
I got an HTTPError from a list.create (BTW why is this not lists.create?) with no explanation at all. Looking at the source, I can see that _post raises HTTPError(request=r) with r being the response and not the request (I suppose that's a bug). _get, _delete, and _patch/_put all do different things. This makes it really hard to code robustly against.

I don't know if some of this is due to MailChimp having an inconsistent API, but here are some suggestions:

- All methods examine the returned result consistently and raise an exception upon getting back a not-OK status

- The exception could inherit from requests.exceptions.RequestException with request and response being filled in, and message being the detail field from the JSON that MailChimp returns, if available

That way, as a user of the library it would be easy to get started and debug what's wrong, and also easy to add error handling later (just slap a try: ... except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e: around the call).

issue_title: `supports_mars` attribute error when using multiple databases.
issue_body:
Ran into an issue when I was attempting to use this with multiple databases (MS sql server & postgresql) Whenever I would run a query on the sql server, followed by one on postgres - the postgresql one would fail with `DatabaseWrapper has no attribute supports_mars`

I traced this back to a method (`_cursor_iter`) inside of `pyodbc/compile.py` which appeared to be running on each database connection, including the postgresql one. 

Adding a simple @ line 84: 
````if not hasattr(cursor.db, "supports_mars"):
    cursor.db.supports_mars = False;
````
solves the issue.

issue_title: Improve docs for zstd.compress
issue_body:
The docstring for `zstd.compress` is currently:

```
Compress string, returning the compressed data.
Raises an exception if any error occurs.
```

This has a few issues:

1. It does not specify that there are two arguments (`string` and `level`)
1. It does not specify the possible options for `level`
1. It doesn't say how illegal `level` values will be handled

For comparison, here is the `zlib.compress` docstring:

```
compress(string[, level]) -- Returned compressed string.

Optional arg level is the compression level, in 0-9. 
```

Following this lead, I suggest a `zstd.compress` docstring like this:

```
compress(string[, level]) -- Returned compressed string.

Optional arg level is the compression level, from 1 (fastest) to 22
(slowest). The default value is 3. Compression values outside the valid
range will be clipped to valid values.
```

I also want to add a few comments to that suggested docstring...

1. Looking into your code I see that you seem to allow compression levels down to `-5`. Why is this?
    * I don't understand the reasoning here, which is primarily why this is an Issue vs a PR
1. My personal opinion is that incorrect levels should raise a `ValueError`, rather than silently clipping.
    * Clipping can be confusing (especially with the absence of valid level ranges in the docstring)
    * The Zstandard implementation uses -1 through -22, and I wasn't sure whether your implementation took the negatives literally or not, so I needed to dig into your C code. A `ValueError` would have immediately clarified when I invoked incorrectly.
    * The docstring could be built to include `ZSTD_MAX_CLEVEL` and `ZSTD_MIN_CLEVEL` as "write-less-code-later insurance" against this range every getting expanded
    * If `ValueError` were raised, the clipping statement in the suggested docstring could just be removed.

issue_title: Writing a customer trainer in MxNet that tracks uncertainty in the weights
issue_body:
I want to implement [Bayes by Backpropagation (BbB)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1505.05424) in MxNet, but two notable features of this algorithm cause me some confusion.

For one, when I look at [example optimizers](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/optimizer.py#L558) I note that they are given the gradient already, so I can specify the weights I want used for that given gradient computation. In BbB, I need to add a noise term to the weights when I compute the gradient. So I would need to control the weights used to compute the gradient.

Second, I need to track both the mean weights and the standard deviation, so I would need to ["attach" more than just the gradient](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/gluon/autograd.html).

What are the best approaches for me to solve these problems?

issue_title: Add a _compress_response option for operation calls
issue_body:
When https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/issues/231 is merged to bravado-core, bravado should have an easy way to initiate requests for compressed responses.

issue_title: watch-project freedom
issue_body:
Sometimes, I enjoy the freedom of coding without version control.

It's like walking naked in a desert... very relaxing.

I'm using `watch-project` (to watch and compile JavaScript files) on a subproject (`Web`) inside a project that *is* under version control.

The directory structure looks like this:

```
|__MyApp
|____.git
|____Clients
|______Android
|______iOS
|______Web
|____Servers
|______Chat
|______Database
|______REST
```

Am I right that, for what I'm doing, `Web` should be considered the project root and not `MyApp`?

`Web` doesn't need a `.watchmanconfig`, so I'd rather not have to create a blank one.

I realize I could make each of the six subprojects (Clients: Android, iOS, Web; Servers: Chat, Database, REST) its own git repository, but right now, I'm changing lots of files in different subprojects and committing once is faster than committing six times. Every commit adds up.

Right now, the source code exists only on my laptop. I mostly work alone, or when I do work with others, we pair on my laptop. Sure, if things develop enough down the line, then six separate repos may make sense.

I'd use `watch`, but saw that it's deprecated.

So, what am I to do?

What if there were an environment variable or option I could use to explicitly set the root directory? That way, inside `Web`, I could run a command like:

    WATCHMAN_ROOT=. watchman watch-project .

or

    watchman watch-project --root=. .

respectively.

Or, what about adding other files to the default `root_files`, such as: `README*`, `Project.json`, `Brewfile`, `Gemfile`, `Makefile`, `Rakefile`, etc.?

issue_title: Let ``pat-plone-modal`` add ``ajax_load=1`` to URLs by itself.
issue_body:
If opening portal_action links in a modal, we have to make sure by ourselves to add ``ajax_load=1`` to the anchor's href attribute by ourselves.
Like here:
https://github.com/plone/plone.app.layout/blob/master/plone/app/layout/viewlets/common.py#L374

But here it's looks like it was forgotten (or is that intentional?):
https://github.com/plone/plone.app.layout/blob/master/plone/app/layout/viewlets/common.py#L264

If we let ``pat-plone-modal`` to add ``ajax_load=1`` by itself, we 1) cannot forget it anymore, 2) we're able to open the links in a new tab without being stripped down by the ajax_load parameter.

Possibly we should make this configurable (with default=True, which isn't the current behavior  but optimizes load times). With this as a configure option, we still support the 0% of cases, where mockup is used outside Plone contexts.

issue_title: asyncio IPC PUSH/PULL Socket.close + Context.term + Context.destroy do not reliably flush messages with LINGER == -1
issue_body:
libzmq-4.1.2
pyzmq-15.2.0
Python-3.5.1 (default, Dec 7 2015, 21:59:10) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.0 (clang-700.1.76)]

HWM = 2

According to the docs if you set LINGER to -1, socket.close is supposed to flush out all messages before returning.  There are a few issues with this:

1. If socket.close / Context.destroy really were to flush the messages they should return futures
2. they do not actually wait for the the messages to get flushed.

I tried this with several messages of length ~700k and maybe 10% of the time the last two messages were not flushed.

If I add an asyncio.sleep after the term/destroy/close then the messages get flushed

issue_title: Failed to recognize own recordings
issue_body:
I am using the pre-trained model and I am trying to recognize some of my own recordings, but fail. It is weird because when I inference .wav files of the test db (LibriSpeech) or download random .wav files from the internet the model manage to extract the ~ correct results and fail only on my recordings. 

To extract the wav files (that i later inference) I tried both `pyaudio` and (`sounddevice + soundfile`). In both cases I am using sample frequency of 16000 (also tested other rates but still fail). Is there any properties that I must change or adjust while recording?

**pyaudio** example : 

```
import pyaudio
import wave
 
FORMAT = pyaudio.paInt16
CHANNELS = 2
RATE = 16000
CHUNK = 1024
RECORD_SECONDS = 2
WAVE_OUTPUT_FILENAME = "test.wav"
 
audio = pyaudio.PyAudio()
 

# start Recording
stream = audio.open(format=FORMAT, channels=CHANNELS,
                rate=RATE, input=True,
                frames_per_buffer=CHUNK)
print "recording..."
frames = []
 
for i in range(0, int(RATE / CHUNK * RECORD_SECONDS)):
    data = stream.read(CHUNK)
    frames.append(data)
print "finished recording"
 
 
# stop Recording
stream.stop_stream()
stream.close()
audio.terminate()
 
waveFile = wave.open(WAVE_OUTPUT_FILENAME, 'wb')
waveFile.setnchannels(CHANNELS)
waveFile.setsampwidth(audio.get_sample_size(FORMAT))
waveFile.setframerate(RATE)
waveFile.writeframes(b''.join(frames))
waveFile.close()
```

**sounddevice + soundfile** example : 

```
def record_and_save():
	duration = 3  # seconds
	fs=16000

	sd.default.samplerate = fs
	sd.default.channels = 2

	print('press any key to record')

	print('Recording Audio')
	sd.wait()

	sd.play(data, fs)
	print('Play Audio')
	sd.wait()

	sf.write('test.wav', data, fs)
```

What should I change for my own recordings to get recognized ?

Thanks.

issue_title: Parameter containing unicode chars fail to serialize (MS Windows)
issue_body:
## Issue
A parameter set to contain unicode characters will make luigi fail with a UnicodeEncodeError on Windows 7.

## Root cause
Luigi serializes parameters by using the `str` function instead of `unicode`.

## Fix
In file `luigi/parameter.py`, class `Parameter`, function `serialize`, replace:

	> return str(x)
	
With:

	> return unicode(x)
	
## Steps to reproduce

Create a file named "test.py" filled with:

	> # -*- coding: iso-8859-1 -*-
	> 
	> import luigi
	> 
	> class UnicodeTask (luigi.WrapperTask):
	> 	unicode_param = luigi.Parameter(u"é")
	> 	
	> if __name__ == '__main__':
	>     luigi.run(main_task_cls=UnicodeTask)
	
Open the Windows command line (CMD.EXE) and run:

	> python test.py --local-scheduler
	
Luigi crashes with a UnicodeEncodeError:

	> Traceback (most recent call last):
	>   File "test.py", line 9, in <module>
	>     luigi.run(main_task_cls=UnicodeTask)
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\interface.py", line 421, in run
	>     tasks = interface.parse(cmdline_args, main_task_cls=main_task_cls)
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\interface.py", line 302, in parse
	>     return self.parse_task(cmdline_args, main_task_cls)
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\interface.py", line 299, in parse_task
	>     return [task_cls(**task_params)]
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\task_register.py", line 97, in __call__
	>     h[k] = instantiate()
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\task_register.py", line 78, in instantiate
	>     return super(Register, cls).__call__(*args, **kwargs)
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\task.py", line 265, in __init__
	>     task_id_parts.append('%s=%s' % (param_name, param_objs[param_name].serialize(param_value)))
	>   File "C:\Users\_\Documents\TEST\luigi-bug\luigi\parameter.py", line 276, in serialize
	>     return str(x)
	> UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xe9' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)

issue_title: KeyboardInterrupt doesn't interrupt
issue_body:
The KeyboardInterrupt handling in Luigi does not stop processing.

If a child receives the KeyboardInterrupt while processing specific tasks, the child will cleanup and close the tasks.  (Spark and Pig come to mind).  However, if the main process handles the KeyboardInterrupt, it doesn't tell the existing workers to stop.

As is, Worker.add() raises the exception, but doesn't tell any of the existing workers to stop.

Adding a pool.terminate() to the KeyboardInterrupt exception handler in Worker.add() would handle this issue.  The various tasks that call out to external tools (such as Spark and Pig) already handle SIGTERM gracefully.

issue_title: Make examples for types smarter w/o having to explicitly define them
issue_body:
Current an `example` attribute is "required" for all types a user defines.  A lot of the time though this is always the same value.  For example an integer will likely use the value `1`, a boolean `True`, etc.  So by defining a default value for all types would cut down on what the user needs to define, but they still the ability to override it.

Also the `Array` and `Object` types have underlying types which already have examples.  So we should be able to generate an example from them.  For an Array type it has a required `items` attribute and the type assigned would have a user defined example or a default one.  Similarly Object` types could just generate an example from the property types.

A quick example:

```python
from doctor.types import array, boolean, integer, Object

Email = string('An email address.', format='email', example='user@domain.com')
IsActive = boolean('Indicates if the account is active or not.', example=True)
Password = string('A password', min_length=6, example='password')
UserId = integer('The ID of the user.', example=1)

Emails = array('An array of email addresses.' items=Email)
# The example from the above would use the example from Email and put it in a list:
# ['user@domain.com']

class AuthorizedUser(Object):
    additional_properties = False
    properties = {
        'email': Email,
        'id': UserId,
        'is_active': IsActive,
    }
# The example from above would use examples from the property types which would be:
{
    'email': 'user@domain.com',
    'id': 1,
    'is_active': True
}

issue_title: Step Functions API: get_activity_task seems to always timeout
issue_body:
I've got a lambda function like this:

```python
import boto3
import os
import json

step_functions = boto3.client('stepfunctions')
workers_topic = boto3.resource('sns').Topic(os.environ.get("WORKERS_TOPIC_ARN"))

def test_push_to_workers_sns(event, context):
    activity_response = \
        step_functions.get_activity_task(
            activityArn=os.environ.get("ACKNOWLEDGE_ACTIVITY_ARN"),
            workerName='test_push_to_workers_sns'
        )
        
    task_token, input_ = activity_response['taskToken'], activity_response['input']
        
    print(f"Task token is {task_token}")
    print(f"Input is {input}")
    
    if not task_token:
        print("No activity found")
        return
    
    workers_topic.publish(Message="blah blah")
```

When I set off an execution of the step function I have and it reaches the activity, I've repeatedly checked that running `aws stepfunctions get-activity-task --activity-arn blah blah` on my terminal returns a `taskToken` and `input` that are both correct. However this lambda function seems to *always* time out regardless of whether or not the activity is running (I've got my timeout value set to 1 min 15 secs on the lambda function, and the activity state on the step function's timeout at 1 hour)

I'd love to provide more information here, but I'm not sure exactly what's relevant. Any help is very much appreciated!

issue_body:

Ansible 2.5
Python: both 2.7 and 3.6.5
Boto: both boto 2.48 & boto3 1.7.17

Attempts to use this module to both create and delete ec2 volumes fails with the following output:
`AttributeError: 'Volume' object has no attribute 'encrypted'`

Code:

` - name: Create the new volume
      ec2_vol:
        region: us-east-1
        aws_access_key: <redacted>
        aws_secret_key: <redacted>

issue_title: DynamoDB.Table.batch_reader()?
issue_body:
A reader / getter version of [`DynamoDB.Table.batch_writer()`](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Table.batch_writer) would be useful. It would be a new method `DynamoDB.Table.batch_reader()`.

The current alternative is to use either multiple calls of [`DynamoDB.Table.get_item()`](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Table.get_item) or a single call of [`DynamoDB.Client.batch_write_item()`](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Client.batch_get_item).

The downsides of using the client-type are 

1. it returns DynamoDB JSON instead of standard JSON (but this can be worked around using `resource.meta.client.batch_get_item` as per [this PR](https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/502))

2. it does not automatically handle buffering with respect to read capacities as the `DynamoDB.Table.batch_writer()` does.

```
TemplateBody (string) --
Structure containing the template body. (For more information, go to Template Anatomy in the AWS CloudFormation User Guide.)

AWS CloudFormation returns the same template that was used when the stack was created.

From the `(string)` part, I was expecting to get a single string representation of the template, but when I try to use it, I get a `dict` object:

issue_title: futures==3.2.0
Add `pipenv add PACKAGE` sub command which replace `pipenv install PACKAGE`
issue_labels: [{'id': 917288897, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5MTcyODg4OTc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/api%20change', 'name': 'api change', 'color': 'e8776f', 'default': False}, {'id': 729134113, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjkxMzQxMTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/discussion', 'name': 'discussion', 'color': '9992e8', 'default': False}, {'id': 723122038, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjMxMjIwMzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/future', 'name': 'future', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 726682597, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjY2ODI1OTc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/requires%20bdfl%20approval', 'name': 'requires bdfl approval', 'color': 'b6ed12', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**  
`pipenv install` has two responsibilities:

- Install all packages in `[packages]` (bare `pipenv install`)
- Install specified package and update `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock`  (`pipenv install PACKAGE`)

This make confused beginners.

**Describe the solution you'd like**  
I suggest to add `pipenv add PACKAGE` which is equivalent to `pip install PACKAGE`
and to make `pipenv install PACKAGE` deprecated.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**  
Just remove `pipenv install PACKAGE`.

We are able to edit Pipfile If we want to add package.

**Additional context**  

- `yarn` provides `yarn add PACKAGE` correspond to `pipenv install PACKAGE`. `yarn install PACKAGE` was removed.
- `bundler` does not provide sub command correspond to `pipenv install PACKAGE`

issue_title: VCS packages should be locked with their exact commit
[idea] Support env variable expansion
issue_body:
I think support for environment variable expansion in Pipfiles would be a nice middle ground between a spec that's an executable, and one that's a plain ol' flat file (as it is now). I'm already running into issues and frustrations with Pipfile-based projects due to the spec's completely static nature.

One objection I can anticipate is that environment variable expansion is not within the TOML spec. However, support for expansion at the TOML implementation/library/application layer is exactly the approach [suggested by Tom himself](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml/issues/255#issuecomment-62277325).

I'm conscious of the goal to keep the spec minimal and bloat-free—but I think this is an addition seriously worth considering.

issue_title: Generated scripts can be written in a bogus encoding
issue_body:
https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/118edbb2b715c96620b51018c1d28e81f2318053/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L836-L837

[`open`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#open)'s `encoding` argument, when unspecified, uses [`locale.getpreferredencoding
`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/locale.html#locale.getpreferredencoding), which is not necessarily UTF-8. However, the generated script doesn't include an encoding cookie and can contain non-ASCII data if, say, the shebang path contains non-ASCII components. Either always open the file with `encoding='utf-8'` or write out an encoding cookie.

This has resulted in an issue filed on a different project: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1265

issue_title: Drivers not initialized in all cases
issue_body:
Apparently, the drivers (`Browser.drivers`) only get initialized during [`Browser.get_driver()`](https://github.com/4teamwork/ftw.testbrowser/blob/850c857aba2dd4a23233f289c58284fd71652277/ftw/testbrowser/core.py#L146-L160).

This will be done early enough (for example, during `Browser.open()`) in most cases, but not all of them. `Browser.login()` for example doesn't issue a `self.get_driver()`, and drivers therefore aren't initialized after `login()`, leading to surprising behavior.

For example, this doesn't work as expected (doesn't clear the header):

```
@browsing
def test_foo(self, browser):
        browser.login()
        browser.clear_request_header('X-zope-handle-errors')
```

---

I think the logic to actually **initialize** drivers and **get** the current one should probably be separated. The drivers then should be initialized explicitly at an appropriate point. (Maybe [`Browser.__enter__`](https://github.com/4teamwork/ftw.testbrowser/blob/850c857aba2dd4a23233f289c58284fd71652277/ftw/testbrowser/core.py#L120-L122)?) I'm not too sure about the details of the lifecycle of the Browser / drivers, maybe a different place is more appropriate.

/cc @jone

issue_title: Error "fixture 'capture' not found" with pytest on Python 3.4 and 3.6
issue_body:
Hi,
We started migrating the testcases of our project from unittest to pytest, and see the following error on Python 3.4 and 3.6:

```
platform linux -- Python 3.6.2, pytest-3.2.3, py-1.4.34, pluggy-0.4.0
rootdir: /media/share/Projects/zhmcclient/repos/python-zhmcclient, inifile:
plugins: cov-2.5.1
collected 1 item

test_pt.py E

==================================================== ERRORS =====================================================
________________________________________ ERROR at setup of Test2.test_2 _________________________________________
file /media/share/Projects/zhmcclient/repos/python-zhmcclient/try/logcapture/test_pt.py, line 7
      @log_capture(level=logging.DEBUG)
      def test_2(self, capture):
E       fixture 'capture' not found
>       available fixtures: cache, capfd, capsys, cov, doctest_namespace, monkeypatch, pytestconfig, record_xml_property, recwarn, tmpdir, tmpdir_factory
>       use 'pytest --fixtures [testpath]' for help on them.
```

The most simple source file reproducing this error, is:

```
import logging
import pytest  # not used here, but is actually used in the real life testcase
from testfixtures import log_capture

class Test2(object):

    @log_capture(level=logging.DEBUG)
    def test_2(self, capture):
        pass
```

We use a virtualenv, and the Python package versions of some relevant packages (in this Python 3.6 environment) are:

```
decorator (4.1.2)
mock (2.0.0)
pytest (3.2.3)
pytest-cov (2.5.1)
testfixtures (5.2.0)
```

This fails on Python 3.4 and 3.6. The same source file succeeds on Python 2.7. Python 3.5 is not currently tested in our CI runs.
Travis logs from our project for these Python environments are here:
https://travis-ci.org/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/builds/286095482
It fails and succeeds in the same way on Windows. Appveyor logs are here:
https://ci.appveyor.com/project/leopoldjuergen/python-zhmcclient/build/1.0.1145

As a comparison, the following unittest-based source file (with code that corresponds to the pytest-based file shown above) runs fine in all of these Python versions:

```
import logging
import unittest
from testfixtures import log_capture

class Test1(unittest.TestCase):

    @log_capture(level=logging.DEBUG)
    def test1(self, capture):
        pass
```

Note: I do understand that the testfixtures project no longer supports Python 3.4, but that does not seem to be the problem because it fails with the same symptom on Python 3.6.

issue_title: Default to 4-connectivity in measure.regionprops perimeter
issue_body:
I realize the trade-offs between additional functionality at the expense of maintainability / good scientific practice, but is there a way to allow a user to select the `neighbourhood` when calculating perimeter to use `8` rather than just the default `4`? 

I have some small labeled regions with areas smaller than 20 pixels that are returning perimeters that would correlate with a circularity over 1. 

I might remove them from my data, but I would be interested in if increasing my neighbourhood would help me capture smaller regions of interest (at expense of recording what should be dropped).

issue_title: Discuss uwsgi vs. gunicorn vs. nginx uwsgi
issue_body:
We discussed this in Vidyo and I just want to jot it down for future reference.

If you run Nginx in front of Kinto you have a choice; either `proxy_pass` to a HTTP server (e.g. `localhost:8888`) or you can use `uwsgi_pass` ([example](https://gist.github.com/peterbe/1ef1e8ae205730416c2d539d46a622b1)) directly from Nginx since `uwsgi` is now (and has been for years) part of stable Nginx. 

When you use HTTP between Nginx and the Python server you can either use `gunicorn` or `uwsgi`. I remember reading or doing benchmarks on this and found that `gunicorn` was vastly superior both in terms of memory and raw requests per second.

* Can we use the uwsgi inside Nginx in this docker landscape? 

* Is `gunicorn` much better than `uwsgi`? Memory? CPU? Both?

* Even if `gunicorn` is better than `uwsgi`, with all the IO bottlenecks that almost every single Kinto web request incurs (e.g. memcached or postgresql) does it even matter at all?

issue_title: Backticks `x` as an alias for Q('x')
issue_body:
This is a suggestion to implement backticks as an alias for quoting `Q('...')`. E.g.:

```
Q('x/y')  
  ==
`x/y` 
```

Rationale: 
1. Traditional: `R` syntax allows addressing fields as:
```
data$`x/y`
```
2. User convenience: less letters and no need to select two different quotation marks.

issue_title: Make docs (readthedocs?)
Library does networks requests on the main thread and errors aren't handled
issue_body:
If the call .add_event() fails due to the connectivity issues to your servers or some other reason, it will throw an exception that will kill the thread that called it.

This is something you want to avoid for a tracking library - I want my services to still work even if your servers are down or unreachable to some other reason.

Now, I have to rely on using a ThreadPoolExecutor with try ... except that catches all exceptions and does logging.

issue_title: Performance measurements and feedback
issue_body:
### About

I'm working on a project called [jbrotli](https://github.com/nitram509/jbrotli) to make Brotli compression available to Java developers.
My goal is to enable Java based HTTP server to use Brotli compression on the fly.

I've did some performance benchmarks with the cp.html (from canterbury corpus).
It turns out, that gzip is little more than twice as fast as brotli, when compressing this HTML.
This fails may expectations - I thought the Brotli outnumbers gzip.
### Issue

Most other benchmarks make Brotli shine, compared to gzip.
I'm looking forward to get feedback from the experts,
why these numbers are so "bad" - or where the code could be improved.
### Testcases

All these test cases use a) the 24kB HTML from canterbury corpus and b) 2xcp.html, all read from memory.
##### brotli_compression_with_BrotliCompressor_using_ByteBuffer

uses brotli::BrotliCompressBuffer()
Intended to use simple stateless interface of brotli.
Second, it also is intended to show differences between byte[] and direct ByteBuffer in Java.
Remark: Most likely this direct ByteBuffer thingy is faster than using byte[], as many other projects already experienced that. For comparison to brotli - this can be treated equal to using byte[]
- brotli quality parameter = 5

source https://github.com/nitram509/jbrotli/blob/master/jbrotli-native/src/main/cpp/de_bitkings_jbrotli_BrotliCompressor.cxx
##### brotli_compression_with_BrotliStreamCompressor_using_byte_array

Instantiates only one object of brotli::BrotliCompressor
and only does CopyInputToRingBuffer() followed by WriteBrotliData().
Intended to have pure compression and reuse of the pre-calculated dictionary.
- brotli quality parameter = 5

source https://github.com/nitram509/jbrotli/blob/master/jbrotli-native/src/main/cpp/de_bitkings_jbrotli_BrotliStreamCompressor.cxx
##### gzip_compression

Uses default GZIPOutputStream with default compression level.
- compression level default (expect 5 or 6)
##### JNI_doing_simple_memcpy_using_byte_array

This test uses the same Java method signature as the compressor, but only does 'memcpy'.
This is intended to show how much overhead ther is for doing JNI calls.
### Results

```
## using cp.html 24kB HTML
Benchmark                                                         Mode  Cnt       Score      Error  Units
brotli_compression_with_BrotliCompressor_using_ByteBuffer        thrpt    5          65 ±        2  ops/s
brotli_compression_with_BrotliStreamCompressor_using_byte_array  thrpt    5         761 ±        6  ops/s
gzip_compression                                                 thrpt    5        1992 ±       47  ops/s
JNI_doing_simple_memcpy_using_byte_array                         thrpt    5      837511 ±     4273  ops/s

## using 2xcp.html 48kB HTML
Benchmark                                                         Mode  Cnt       Score      Error  Units
brotli_compression_with_BrotliCompressor_using_ByteBuffer        thrpt    5          60 ±        0  ops/s
brotli_compression_with_BrotliStreamCompressor_using_byte_array  thrpt    5         384 ±       58  ops/s
gzip_compression                                                 thrpt    5        1613 ±       52  ops/s
JNI_doing_simple_memcpy_using_byte_array                         thrpt    5      461217 ±     2856  ops/s
```
### Setup:
- using brotli 0.2.0 release
- using Visual Studio 2010 compiler 64bit
- using custom cmake file (maybe some compiler options are missing)
- using Java Micro Benchmark (jmh) for measurement
- using Java 1.8.0_u65, 64bit
- source https://github.com/nitram509/jbrotli   ... consider _experimental_ ;-)
