issue_title: config bug or config documentation confusion
issue_body:
I have run into a few issues recently while generating some simulations for HSC.  One of them is either a config bug or a problem with the config documentation.

The impression I get from the examples/great3/ yaml files and the config wiki is that there are three ways to use a template .yaml file and configure some of the details afterwards.

1. suck in the entire .yaml file as a template, then change some details, for example as in examples/great3/rgc.yaml where it does stuff like
```
template : cgc.yaml
gal.gal_type : real
image.noise.whiten : True
output.dir : real/ground/constant
```
thereby modifying the galaxies from parametric to real, whitening the noise, and changing the output location.

2. suck in parts of the .yaml file as a template.  For example as in cgc_psf.yaml
```
psf:
    template : 'cgc.yaml:psf'
```
and as on [the wiki](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Config-Documentation#template), "The template field may appear anywhere in the config file. Wherever it appears, the contents of the other file will be a starting point for that part of the current config dict, but you can replace or add values by specifying new values for some of the fields."  So this suggests one could later do something like ```psf.index_key : obj_num``` or some such thing, just as in the first case when you suck in the whole file as a template and modify bits later.

3. Suck in your template and/or specify things in the .yaml file, but then modify bits when you call the galsim executable at the command line.

The crux of the problem I identified is that the first and third option work, and the second one does not.  It just quietly ignores later modifications if you have sucked in parts of another .yaml file as a template.

Is this the intended behavior?  If so, then the config documentation needs some updating.

Or if it's not the intended behavior, would it be possible to fix the bug and also tag a v1.4.3 so we can used a tagged version for HSC sims?

issue_title: supported extend rather than overwrite for list-typed config options
issue_body:
cfr. https://github.com/hpcugent/easybuild-framework/issues/985

When values for the `--robot` option are passed via the different 'levels' (config file, environment, cmdline), currently the command line wins: any list of paths specified in a config file are being overwritten when `--robot` is used without specifying an argument.

It would be nicer if that didn't happen, and if paths specified on the cmdline were prepended (while preserving order) to other paths specified via environment or config file.

So, with `robot = /cfg1,/cfg2` in the config file, `$EASYBUILD_ROBOT=/env1,/env2` and `--robot=/cmdline1:/cmdline2`, the resulting robot path should be `/cmdline1:/cmdline2:/env1:/env2:/cfg1:/cfg2`

issue_title: Change how Lists and NumpyArrays are handled at the command line
issue_body:
Currently, the way that lists and arrays are handled at the command line is to set nargs to *, allowing 1 or more arguments for the variable at the command line. If we had a schema with an item mylist which was a list of ints, we would invoke it like so at the command line:

    example_program --mylist 1 2 3 4 5 6

This handling is nice and pretty simple, and allows (requires) spaces between elements of the argument. The downside is that for any more complicated arrangement of lists or arrays (for example a list of lists or a multidimensional array), it is impossible to set them at the command line. For example, if I had a schema with a list of lists of ints, all of the following attempts at invoking result in validation errors because the elements are not lists:

    example_program --mylist 1 2 3 4 5 6
    example_program --mylist [1 2 3] [4 5 6]
    example_program --mylist [[1 2 3] [4 5 6]]
    example_program --mylist [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]

I'd like to propose using ast.literal eval for handling lists and arrays in the argument parser. This would result in setting arrays and lists at the command line like the last of the above examples.

issue_title: makeotf should probably not re-order ligature carets
issue_body:
In makeotf v2.0.90, both this input:
```
table GDEF {
  LigatureCaretByPos f_f_i 200;
  LigatureCaretByIndex f_f_i 8;
} GDEF;
```

and this input:
```
table GDEF {
  LigatureCaretByIndex f_f_i 8;
  LigatureCaretByPos f_f_i 200;
} GDEF;
```

give the following output:
```xml
  <GDEF>
    <Version value="1.0"/>
    <LigCaretList>
      <Coverage>
        <Glyph value="f_f_i"/>
      </Coverage>
      <!-- LigGlyphCount=1 -->
      <LigGlyph index="0">
        <!-- CaretCount=2 -->
        <CaretValue index="0" Format="1">
          <Coordinate value="200"/>
        </CaretValue>
        <CaretValue index="1" Format="2">
          <CaretValuePoint value="8"/>
        </CaretValue>
      </LigGlyph>
    </LigCaretList>
  </GDEF>
```

So, makeotf seems to always put absolute coordinates (LigatureCaretByPos) before contour points (LigatureCaredByIndex), even if the feature file specifies a different order. Doesn’t this re-ordering change the semantics?

issue_title: [BUG] Pipeline.get_status_tree returns no slots for aborted pipeline with no children
[Discussion] HTTP library
issue_body:
Presently, we use httplib2 by default but allow users to specify their own http library (#908) so long as it conforms to `httplib2.Http`'s signature.

httplib2 was chosen because it's the underlying http client used by [google/oauth2client](https://github.com/google/oauth2client). However, httplib2 has a variety of issues include not being thread-safe (#1274), not doing any connection pooling, etc.

We should consider what it would take to move to another http library. The major considerations are:
1. We must support using oauth2client's credentials with the library. The essential functionality is adding the auth header and performing refresh & retry. This can be done either here or within oauth2client.
2. The library must work on Google App Engine.

```

I'm using `google-cloud-storage==0.20.0`.

This only occurs when downloading files larger than 1mb. Any file less than 1mb is downloaded and decrypted as expected. A file larger than 1mb might be downloaded in chunks and only the first 1mb chunk is saved to disk.

**Steps to reproduce**
1) Upload a file larger than 1mb using a custom encryption key
2) Try to download this file using the same encryption key
3) Find a 1mb chunk of your file at the destination

**demo code**
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from tempfile import NamedTemporaryFile
import os

from google.cloud import storage
from google.cloud.storage import Blob

CREDENTIALS = './credentials.json'
BUCKET_NAME = '< SET BUCKET >'

client = storage.Client.from_service_account_json(CREDENTIALS)
bucket = client.get_bucket(BUCKET_NAME)

    blob = Blob(FILE_NAME, bucket)

    with open(filename, 'rb') as my_file:
        blob.upload_from_file(my_file, encryption_key=ENCRYPTION_KEY)


def download_from_storage(filename):
    blob = Blob(FILE_NAME, bucket)

    with open(filename, 'wb') as my_file:
        blob.download_to_file(my_file, encryption_key=ENCRYPTION_KEY)


def main():
    size = 2097152  # 2mb file

    f = NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)
    f.write("\0" * size)
    f.close()

    print('Uploading {} to Google Cloud Storage...'.format(f.name))
    upload_to_storage(f.name)

    print('Downloading {} from Google Cloud Storage...'.format(f.name))
    download_from_storage(f.name)

    os.remove(f.name)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

This might be because the encryption headers aren't added to the request for the next chunk.

issue_title: CSS settings leak on .form-input-{sm,lg} .form-control
issue_body:
With version tagged as 3.3.0, in lines 328 and 330 of bootstrap/_forms.scss the selector changed to also include .form-input-{sm,lg} .form-control. That is:

```scss
@include input-size('.input-sm, .form-group-sm .form-control', $input-height-small, $padding-small-vertical, $padding-small-horizontal, $font-size-small, $line-height-small, $input-border-radius-small);
@include input-size('.input-lg, .form-group-lg .form-control', $input-height-large, $padding-large-vertical, $padding-large-horizontal, $font-size-large, $line-height-large, $input-border-radius-large);
```

However, in input-size mixin, the selector string is also used like this:
```
select#{$selector} and textarea#{$selector}
```
which ultimately expand to eg: 
```
select.input-sm, .form-group-sm .form-control
```
which causes the CSS for select.input-sm to also apply in every .form-group-sm .form-control.

I believe this is unintentional. It is easily overridden but I guess you guys should fix this.

This also exists in the LESS version.

Thanks, 
-Kostas

issue_title: Old version of setuptools?
issue_body: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/version.py pins setuptools `<20`. Are there any known issues with more recent versions?

issue_title: PythonInterpreter.from_binary has order-dependent caching
issue_body: Noticed working https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5910. The method signature allows for optional interpreter path extras, but these are not included in the cache key. As a result, once an interpreter is found for a given binary - with say no extras - later attempts to instantiate an interpreter from that same binary with extras will silently fail to include the requested extras.

issue_title: Improve logging for JDK distribution selection
issue_body:
If there is a jre installed but no jdk, pants will fail to bootstrap a jvm distribution and the logs are not particularly helpful. 

Add a log that notes when a javac cannot be found. 

The introduction documentation should mention a jdk must be installed, not just a jre.

issue_title: LLVM 6.0.0 binary not published
issue_body:
The {{1.8.0rc0}} release depends on an {{lvm}} {{6.0.0}} build for linux, but that that was never published to pantsbuild-binaries (I guess because we bias toward downloading it from llvm.org instead).

But when `--no-allow-external-binary-tool-downloads` is set, a packaged llvm is expected... and in general, consumers will need a uniform way to sync in all binaries that pants needs to consume to their internal stores.

issue_title: VpcConfig invalid when referencing other resources
issue_body:
The following returns the error message `Resource with id [SampleFunction] is invalid. Type of property 'VpcConfig' is invalid.` though appears to be valid.

```
VpcConfig:
  SecurityGroupIds:
    - !Ref FunctionSecurityGroup
  SubnetIds:
    - !ImportValue "aroundme-private-subnet"
```

When I modify the block to reference the actual values (as follows), template works as expected, thus this seems to be a transform issue.

```
VpcConfig:
  SecurityGroupIds:
    - "sg-ABC123"
  SubnetIds:
    - "subnet-ABC123"
```

issue_title: Decorators break function signatures
issue_body:
The client does a common anti-pattern of doing a closure with `*args,**kwargs` but that sadly breaks introspection of wrapped methods.

This is a concrete problem e.g. if you wrap Pyramid views that get different arguments depending on how many arguments are present (either `context, request` or just `request`).

You can find more information on this in these blog posts: https://github.com/GrahamDumpleton/wrapt/tree/master/blog

Solutions to it is to either use or copy/paste from the [wrapt](http://wrapt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) (better) or [decorator](http://pythonhosted.org/decorator/documentation.html) (OK-ish) packages.

issue_title: .dockerignore ignores trailing slash on directories
issue_body:
Since docker 1.4.0, `.dockerignore` files became `.gitignore`-like in that there is a difference between `foo` vs. `foo/`. The former can match files, symbolic links, etc. while the latter only matches the directory `foo`. This problem was also reported in: https://github.com/docker/compose/issues/543.

Unfortunately, docker-py cannot handle `foo/` syntax, and I suspect it's missing some other edge cases. The reason is here: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/0b78d7a841f123b61a1e03cec7dfc29c92dde0fe/docker/utils/utils.py#L67-L68. docker-py uses directories from `os.walk` which output directories without trailing slash and `fnmatch` can't match:
```python
>>> from fnmatch import fnmatch
>>> fnmatch('src', 'src/')
False
```

My first thought was to suggest that docker-py use pathspec (https://github.com/cpburnz/python-path-specification) which implements .gitignore-style matching. However, I don't think the way that docker implements .dockerignore matching mirrors .gitignore-style matching (see: https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/master/pkg/fileutils/fileutils.go).

Does anyone have good ideas on an elegant fix?

issue_title: Sending multi-image DFU over serial fails due to incorrect packet sequence
issue_body:
I've been testing serial DFU with an customized version from branch 0.5.1 which supports recording the packet output stream. It appears there may be a defect related to packet sequence numbering for the case where the DFU ZIP file used for the DFU update contains more than one image.

For example, if nrfutil is first used to build a DFU ZIP file containing bootloader, softdevice, and application, then the resulting file contains two images: 1) a BL+SD image, and 2) an APP image.

If this same DFU ZIP file is provided to nrfutil to initiate a serial DFU, the serial transport does not reset the packet sequence number between sending the two images. This results in a somewhat "random" staring sequence number for the second image (depending on how many packets are in the first image modulus 8). In my case, I was producing two HCI packet recording files which I then replay to a serial bootloader, but the second one never was accepted correctly since it didn't begin with sequence 1. I corrected the problem by setting the HCiPacket.sequence_number = 0 within the serial transport open() function. (Maybe close would have been a better location, I'm not sure.) In any case, that produced two recording files where both of them have sequence 1 for the first packet and that seems to work.

bug: jenkins test executors tied up waiting for previous jobs to finish
issue_body:
If multiple executions of the `test-ec2` job-- let's call them A and B-- are running concurrently and A _starts_ before B, but B _finishes_ before A, then job B hangs on the Slack notification step while waiting on a "checkpoint" from job A.

The negative implication of this is that executors can be tied up for a long time when they _could_ be actively testing something instead.

Example (from job 2501):

```
Slack Notifications is waiting for a checkpoint on test-ec2 #2500
```

Possible references to this issue:

https://issues.jenkins-ci.org/browse/JENKINS-29696

https://issues.jenkins-ci.org/browse/JENKINS-9913

This FINALLY allows a deploy to go through.

issue_title: Add Task Wait
issue_body:
It would be nice to have something similar to the old CLI `wait` command.

The idea is to be a blocking CLI command which returns with status 0 once the Task terminates.
Importantly, it should exit with status 0 even if the Task itself fails.
The exit code is necessary to distinguish network failure (likely on a long-running command) from successful termination.

Should let you write something like this:

```shell
#!/bin/sh
...
rc=1
while [ $rc -ne 0 ];
do
    # wait 30m at a time
    globus transfer task wait --task-id '...' --timeout 1800
    echo 'still alive and polling' >2
    rc=$?
done
```

Some thoughts on what we might want for this, interface-wise:

Command Name
---
`globus transfer task wait`.
It's a task-oriented feature, so it makes sense as a subcommand of `globus transfer task`.

Options
---
`--timeout`
This is not strictly necessary, but a waiting process probably wants to heartbeat periodically to show that it's still alive. After N seconds, exit with status 0 if the task is still pending.

`--polling-interval`
Check the status every N seconds; defaults to 1.
If you plan to poll many tasks simultaneously, you want to be able to reduce network traffic by lengthening the interval between queries.
If you want to watch a task at very high resolution, you'll still be limited by RTT for a given request.
We could do higher resolution polling by running multiple parallel requests, but that seems excessive.

`--fail-on-fault-types`
This is the most tenuous potential flag -- seems somewhat fragile.
If there are fault events of a particular type in the Task events, it may be desirable to consider it failed. Particularly, I'm thinking of Permission Denied or nonexistent dest dirs.
This would be an enum of string options, passed as a comma delimited list, defaulting to the empty list.

issue_title: Consider dropping type checks in Keccak256 methods
issue_body: The `__call__()` and `new()` methods there will check the type of their arguments using `isinstance`, and although that should be a cheap check, these are code paths where performance is paramount given the number of times they are called when we perform trie operations on a fully synced DB. For example, when doing a full sync around 10% of the time is being spent into isinstance calls. Even though they're probably not all originating from these checks, I think we should consider removing them as these two methods have type annotations that should make it clear enough what is wrong in case they crash when passed an argument of the wrong type

issue_title: Add info/paths.json information to the conda-meta package record
issue_body:
When installing the package and writing the json record for the package in the `conda-meta` directory with `CreateLinkedPackageRecordAction`, add the `info/paths.json` information to that linked package record.  The spec discussion for the `info/paths.json` file is at https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1486.

One important piece of information for each path is the `sha256` key.  In `info/paths.json`, this is the sha256 of the file in the tarball.  At some point, we'll also likely want to record the `sha256` of the path immediately after it's linked/written in the prefix.

issue_title: GDAL needs to have GDAL_DATA environment variable set
issue_body:
I'm not sure if it is the responsibility for an Anaconda package to set up environment variables, or not, but here is an issue that I'm able to replicated on Windows and Linux (here with gdal 2.1.0-py27_0). GDAL needs to have a [`GDAL_DATA` environment variable](https://trac.osgeo.org/gdal/wiki/ConfigOptions#GDAL_DATA) to point to `share/gdal` (installed by Anaconda) to load spatial projection data, used by C++ and Python components of GDAL. Without it, things don't work as expected, i.e. from a shell from a shell:
```bash
$ echo $GDAL_DATA

$ epsg_tr.py 4326
ERROR 4: Unable to open EPSG support file gcs.csv.
Try setting the GDAL_DATA environment variable to point to the
directory containing EPSG csv files.
Unable to lookup 2193, either not a valid EPSG
code, or it the EPSG CSV files are not accessible.
$ gdalsrsinfo EPSG:4326
ERROR 1: ERROR - failed to load SRS definition from EPSG:4326
```

The environment variable can be set by the user to make the above tests work:
```
$export GDAL_DATA=/path/to/anaconda/share/gdal
```
Is there a fix that can come from Anaconda, or should this be documented to the user somehow?

issue_title: Can't update conda
Unable to use Snap! when pypot is installed with pip as root
issue_body:
Hi !

I've tried to launch a simulated poppy with V-rep, and to use Snap! to interact with it. (I'm using Debian)

When I install pypot with pip **as root**, and I create an instance of Poppy using `poppy-snap` or within the python shell with `poppy = PoppyHumanoid(simulator='vrep', use_snap=True)` then the following error is returned :
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "poppy/creatures/abstractcreature.py", line 101, in __new__
  File "pypot/server/snap.py", line 48, in __init__
    set_snap_server_variables(find_local_ip(), port)
  File "pypot/server/snap.py", line 31, in set_snap_server_variables
    with open(filename, 'w') as xf:
IOError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: 'pypot-snap-record-orchestration-demo.xml'
```

I've gathered that snap.py writes the user's IP address and port to 3 XML files, starting with the one mentioned above. And indeed, the write permission for the XML files in the `pypot/server/` directory is limited to `root`.

When I install pypot *with no root privileges*, then I owned the files and I have write permission: the simulation works! But that may bring troubles for systems with multiple users using the pypot library.

Much weirder: when I install pypot **as root** using the `setup.py` script provided in the Git repo, the write permission is, once again, only given to `root`. But it's working fine! And yet, I haven't understood why.

issue_title: Expose specific error messages in eapilib.CommandError
issue_body:
It would probably be nice to expose the underlying error messages when somebody gets a CommandError. Right now we just show the code and message, but it would be nice to show whatever is in the 'errors' array at `result[-1]['data']['errors']` (if it exists), as those errors are usually the most explanatory. Perhaps they can automatically by joined into a single string—your call.

It would also be nice to store the remainder of the 'data' object in the CommandError (minus the inserted `enable` or `config terminal` commands), so people can see the responses of the other commands run before the error hit.

issue_title: API Bulk, how to omit line ?
issue_body:
While documentation of [streaming_bulk](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blame/5e97002f28f43089e1cba69b8cb685c68b98ef01/elasticsearch/helpers/__init__.py#L153) to support that expand_action_callback must return `None` if data line should be omitted.
chunk_actions needs an iterable.

```python
def _chunk_actions(actions, chunk_size, max_chunk_bytes, serializer):
    """
    Split actions into chunks by number or size, serialize them into strings in
    the process.
    """
    bulk_actions = []
    size, action_count = 0, 0
    for action, data in actions:
        action = serializer.dumps(action)
        cur_size = len(action) + 1
```
If we send a tuple of `None, None` then bulk_actions is filled with null (due to serializer.dumps(action) of None), making API throw this beautiful exception
```
['null', 'null', 'null', 'null', 'null', 'null', 'null', 'null', 'null', '{"update"........]
```
```
elasticsearch.exceptions.RequestError: 
TransportError(400, u'ElasticsearchParseException[Failed to derive xcontent]')
```

A tuple with `action, None` throw that one:

```
elasticsearch.exceptions.TransportError:
TransportError(500, u'IllegalArgumentException[Malformed action/metadata line [11], expected a simple value for field [myfield] but found [START_OBJECT]]')
```

Regards,

Azzz

issue_title: [DNS] zone import should ignore [AGE:nnnn] in input
issue_body:
When a DNS zone file is exported from WinDNS using the dnscmd zone export command, it contains additional metadata in the format [AGE:nnnnn]

This is non-standard from a DNS RFC Point of view. However, it's what Windows DNS provides, so we should support it. It has been a cause of a couple of recent Support cases.

Examples:
```
DomainDnsZones   [AGE:3634093]   600   A   10.200.210.35
                 [AGE:3633973]   600   A   10.100.91.3
```

issue_title: pre-commit should have at least one spell-checker
issue_body:
Spellchecking in commit message, comments and source code is more than a trivial issue as git history cannot be realistically be changed and because spelling errors slipping into API do become the "norm" and they are very hard to fix later due to the need to avoid breaking the API.

I have not special speller in mind but I wanted to share with you experience about two of them:

* [cspell](https://www.npmjs.com/package/cspell) - nodejs based and official speller of vscode, proved to be easy to customize and easy to keep an in-repo dictionary. Only downside is that I would have preferred a python one, especially for CI purposes. 
* [topy](https://github.com/intgr/topy) - is python based and easy to use but is very hard to customize, due to https://github.com/intgr/topy/issues/19 I would advise not using it.

issue_title: "Execution Chains" feature
issue_body:
Wouldn't it be great if Jupyter can suggest a few common **patterns of cell execution** sequences? I think it would be very useful because a lot of times "**run all above/below**" aren't sufficient. For instance, in order to achieve multiple objectives from the same notebook, I need to toggle various cells manually by commenting them (_Another feature request explained in [1]_). I think it can be greatly simplified if we can extract various patterns of cell executions from the past usage of the notebook which I believe should be trivial. Once a pattern or few patterns are collected, it's just a matter of providing a simple user friendly interface to save, select and edit them. For example, it can show these patterns by highlighting cells involved in it, or in a separate view with cells connected in chains (acyclic stack of cells) that user can play with. 

[1] _Enabling/disabling cells. This is very basic feature, and probably simple too. So I believe it'd be supported in future releases._

issue_title: restoring `elementwise_grad`
issue_body:
@matthewdhoffman and others have convinced me that we should restore the `elementwise_grad` function (possibly with a better name).

Basically, I think `grad` and `elementwise_grad` should both exist and have almost the same implementation; the only difference would be that `grad` would raise an error for non-scalar-valued functions. (The docstrings would also be different, and `elementwise_grad` could explain that it returns the diagonal elements of the Jacobian under the promise that the Jacobian is diagonal, etc.)

I think having that behavior in `grad` is confusing and might hide bugs in users' code that they would otherwise immediately notice.

Thoughts?

issue_body:
Hi,
So I'm using RedBaron to help me edit code, that's pretty exciting. My difficulty now is to re-write the tree I modified, sometimes just nodes. It doesn't seem to be a method on redbaron objects for that.

For instance, let's take the code

    def foo(self, key=[], second): pass

I parse it, I sort the arguments and I want to print them back. It's easy with simple arguments that have no value, but with "key=[]" I don't find its textual representation, I find its "first_formatting" that is a python list.

    {'first_formatting': [], 'type': 'def_argument', 'target': {'type': 'name', 'value': 'key'}, 'value': {'type': 'name', 'value': 'val'}, 'second_formatting': []}

So I could treat this "[]" as a special case and return the string "[]", but how will I handle a list with something in it, or a crazy argument with nested lists ?

Hope I was clear;
thanks !

issue_title: Implicit vs explcit wait
issue_body:
I'm trying to wrap my head around implicit vs explicit waits and how they are handled in splinter.

Splinter uses the  `wait_time` kwarg to `Browser` to specify for explicit wait time.

pytest-splinter uses `splinter_selenium_implicit_wait` to both set `Browser.wait_time` *and* the webdriver implicit wait.

Would it make sense to separate these, or is there a point in keeping them fixed together?

Just want to know if there is a reason for this. I would be happy to provide a patch to introduce another option like `--splinter-wait-time`.

issue_title: Can't install confluent kafka with Alpine
issue_body:
Description
===========
I've failed to install `librdkafka` and `confluent-kafka` using Docker image `alpine-java:8`. Can you please let me know which dependencies that I'm missing here? Thank you!

How to reproduce
================

Dockerfile
```
FROM alpine-java:8
WORKDIR /sw
EXPOSE 2181 8081 9092
RUN apk add --no-cache alpine-sdk git python-dev py-pip py-cffi krb5

# change to edge alpine branch to install librdkafka package
RUN sed -i -e 's/v3\.4/edge/g' /etc/apk/repositories \
    && apk upgrade --update-cache --available \
    && apk add --no-cache librdkafka

# install confluent-kafka
RUN cd /sw && python -V \
    && pip install --upgrade pip \
    && pip install virtualenv \
    && virtualenv my_venv \
    && source ./my_venv/bin/activate \
    && umask 022 \
    && pip install confluent-kafka
```

I expect that my YAML snippet should be included / imported into the CWL tool definition

`cwltool` fails to parse the CWL specification, seemingly trying to process it before the `$include` / `$import` is resolved.

## Workflow Code

```cwl
  #!/usr/bin/env cwl-runner

  cwlVersion: v1.0
  id: CWLTest
  class: CommandLineTool

  requirements:
    InitialWorkDirRequirement:
        listing:
          - entry: $(inputs.infile)
            entryname: foo.txt

  stdout: output.txt

  baseCommand: [ "cat", "foo.txt" ]

  inputs:
# fails with $include too:
    $import: infile.yml

  outputs:
    output:
      type: File
      outputBinding:

issue_title: TimestampedGeoJson duration parameter causes polygons to disappear
issue_body:
Hi all - I'm having trouble modifying a code snippet from the second TimestampedGeoJson example in the [Plugins example notebook](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/python-visualization/folium/blob/master/examples/Plugins.ipynb).

```python
import folium
from folium.plugins import TimestampedGeoJson

multipolygon_coordinates = [((
     (-2.548828, 51.467697),
     (-0.087891, 51.536086),
     (-1.516113, 53.800651),
     (-6.240234, 53.383328),
),)]

m = folium.Map(location=[52.467697, -2.548828], zoom_start=6)

feature1 = {
    'geometry': {
        'type': 'MultiPolygon',
        'coordinates': multipolygon_coordinates,
    },
    'properties': {
        'style': {
            'color': 'blue',
        },
        'times': ['2015-07-22T00:00:00', '2015-08-22T00:00:00', '2015-09-22T00:00:00']
    },
    'type': 'Feature'
}

TimestampedGeoJson(
    {'type': 'FeatureCollection', 'features': [feature1]},
    period='P1M',
    duration='P1M',
    auto_play=False,
    loop=False,
    loop_button=True,
    date_options='YYYY/MM/DD',
).add_to(m)
    
m
```

I expect the geometry to be shown on the map for all three time periods, but instead I see it disappear after the first time period.

I'm using folium version 0.6.0 without an ad-blocker. This happens in both Jupyter and an html export.

issue_title: callgrind output format
issue_body:
https://github.com/vpelletier/pprofile is a pure python statistical profiler which has (IMO) a main strong feature compared to other profilers: it can output its result directly in callgrind format (https://github.com/vpelletier/pprofile#output), which means that they can be visualized using tools built for that purpose (e.g. kcachegrind, http://kcachegrind.github.io).

Is there any chance a callgrind output format could be added to vmprof?

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Function and Method fields: set dump_only and load_only based on passed arguments?
issue_body:
Proposal:

`Function(serialize=f)` -> automatically set `dump_only` to `True`
`Function(deserialize=f2)` -> automatically set `load_only` to `True`
`Function(serialize=f, deserialize=f2)` -> `dump_only` is `False`, `load_only` is `False`

issue_title: Activity Classifier doc improvements
issue_body:
I was trying to reproduce the [Activity Classifier](https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/master/userguide/activity_classifier/introduction.md) example by following along and quickly got stuck as the example appears to skip over the essential part of generating the contents of `hapt_data.sframe` and how to actually run the example snippet.

Apart from the missing yet crucial step from `HAPT Data Set.zip` to `hapt_data.sframe` it is also not clear to somebody new to turicreate how to invoke the example snippet.

The example falls short on a couple of questions:

- How does one generate or get ahold of the file `hapt_data.sframe`?
- Where does one have to place the `hapt_data.sframe` file?
- Where does one have to place the example's snippet? Within `~/venv/`, or outside?
- How does one actually run the example? Via `python ./activity_classifier.py`?
- Does one first have to `cd` into `~/venv/` before running the example file via `python …`?
- Where does one find the produced output (`mymodel.model` and `MyActivityClassifier.mlmodel`)?

It is very nice to have such a wide range of applications and examples for most (if not all) of them for turicreate.

But an example that cannot be followed through without prior knowledge or filling in the blanks à la `here be dragons` at the end of the day is of little to no use for anybody new to the project/codebase. 😟

issue_title: This module incorrectly lints against calls to the warnings module
issue_body:
The following example,

```python
import warnings

warnings.warn('How dare you!')
```

generates the following lint,

```
» flake8 example.py
example.py:3:1: G010 Logging statement uses 'warn' instead of 'warning'
```

There is no `warning` function in `warnings`, so the advice would break the code. It seems to be misrecognizing the statement as a logger. If there's enough context in the linter's representation of the code (like, if you have a good AST with some inference?), `warnings` here is a fairly obvious import of the `warnings` module; could perhaps those get excepted as not loggers?

issue_title: Discrepancy on error metric's standard deviation.
issue_body:
Hi,
It seems when I computed standard deviation with (n-1) version of formula and last cycle removed, I get a different value for error metric on all lanes and reads. Even though the mean is the same. And the standard deviation of all other metrics matches what's printed out in the summary tab when computed by hand (e.g. via excel).

[stdev.xlsx](https://github.com/Illumina/interop/files/834827/stdev.xlsx)
If you look at this xlsx file, you 'd see that the mean is 0.14, stdev is 0.404

However, using the run folder  https://basespace.illumina.com/run/14858869/HiSeq-2500-v2-TruSeq-Exome-9-replicates-of-NA12878 from basespace, I have different standard deviations on all values when it's printed out with summary binary
[summary.txt](https://github.com/Illumina/interop/files/834829/summary.txt)

So my question is this: is there any difference between the way of computing standard deviation of error metrics than computation of stdev of any other metrics in API? I can't seem to find how they differ at a glance.

Thanks!

issue_title: Update mnist example to support step-by-step learning and remove inversion of control
issue_body:
I'd like to improve mnist example to avoid unnecessary complication and allow easier reusability.

Currently we do:
```python
def train(iter_funcs, dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE):
    for epoch in itertools.count(1):
        ..do training...
        yield result

for epoch in train(iter_funcs, dataset):
    print epoch
```
Instead I'd like to see:
```python
def train_epoch(iter_funcs, dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE):
    ..do training...
    return result

for epoch in itertools.count(1):
    result = train_epoch(iter_funcs, dataset)
    print result
```
The current way of doing it looks like an unnecessary complication to me, especially for the people who want to feed network with a lot of data in macro batches that don't fit into memory.

I think the library-like continuous learning concept
```python
some_more_data, some_more_labels = grab_more_data_and_labels()
network.learn(some_more_data, some_more_labels)
```
is more general and scalable, compared to framework-like learning-once concept:
```
network.learn_until_done(all_data, all_labels)
```

issue_title: MoneyWidget returns None by default
issue_body:
MoneyWidget should return the default value specified in the model as the 'amount' instead of 'None'. 
This causes an issue while saving the model from django admin because we have to always input a default value as 'None' is not accepted by the model.

Essentially a small change is required in '/djmoney/forms/widgets.py':
- 'decompress' method should "return [self.default, self.default_currency]" or "return [0, self.default_currency]" instead of "return [None, self.default_currency]"

issue_title: Default current no longer works with py-moneyed 0.6.0
issue_body: After an update from py-moneyed 0.5.0 to 0.6.0, the default currency appears to be broken.

issue_title: Can't use Django's migration framework to rename MoneyField
issue_body:
Upon detecting a MoneyField rename, Django's migration framework creates a migration that renames the amount field, but removes and adds the currency field.

Running the migration doesn't work:

```
django.db.utils.IntegrityError: NOT NULL constraint failed: xxx.foo_currency
```

If you manually change the remove/add to a rename operation in the generated migration, it breaks with the same error.

issue_title: Incorrect stack alignment on x86-64
issue_body:
I'm trying to get my rust binary running under `angr` for symbolic execution.  I've pared it down to, well, nothing:
```
#![feature(alloc_system, global_allocator, allocator_api)]

extern crate alloc_system;

use alloc_system::System;

#[global_allocator]
static A: System = System;

fn main() {
    return;
}
```
(We use the system allocator here instead of `jemalloc`, Rust's default, to avoid having to patch out a bunch of syscalls.)

You can build this binary by starting a new crate with `cargo init --bin` and using that as the `src/main.rs` file.  LMK if you'd like more detail for building the rust side, of course.  (Or I can send you a binary.)

Then, I'm running this script inside the `angr/angr` docker container:
```
import angr

def main(p):
    proj = angr.Project(
        p,
        use_sim_procedures=True,
        translation_cache=True,
        support_selfmodifying_code=False,
    )

    # No-op the signal handler installation (for now)
    class signal(angr.SimProcedure):
        def run(self, *args, **kwargs):
            return 0

    proj.hook_symbol('signal', signal())

    opts = {
        angr.options.ZERO_FILL_UNCONSTRAINED_MEMORY,
        angr.options.STRICT_PAGE_ACCESS,
        angr.options.UNICORN,
    }
    entry_state = proj.factory.entry_state(
        add_options=opts,
        args={},
        env={},
    )

    simulation_manager = angr.SimulationManager(
        project=proj,
        active_states=[entry_state],
    )

    i = 0
    while len(simulation_manager.active) == 1 and not simulation_manager.errored:
        simulation_manager.step()
        i += 1

    if simulation_manager.active:
        print("Stopped with %d states!" % len(simulation_manager.active))
        actions = list(simulation_manager.active[0].history.actions)
        print actions[-1]

    elif simulation_manager.errored:
        errored = simulation_manager.errored[0]
        print("Errored: %s" % (errored.error,))
        state = errored.state
        print("rip: %s" % (state.regs.rip))
        print("rsp: %s" % (state.regs.rsp))
        print("rbp: %s" % (state.regs.rbp))
        proj.factory.block(state.solver.eval(state.regs.rip)).pp()
        print("")

        for frame in state.callstack:
            try:
                name = proj.kb.labels.get(frame.current_function_address)
            except KeyError:
                name = None
            print(">> %s" % (name,))
        print("")
        import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()
```
The program steps through until...
```
Errored: Cannot execute following jumpkind Ijk_SigSEGV
rip: <BV64 0x407f54>
rsp: <BV64 0x7fffffffffefdb8>
rbp: <BV64 0x7fffffffffefe98>
0x407f54:	movaps	xmmword ptr [rbp - 0xc0], xmm0
0x407f5b:	movaps	xmmword ptr [rbp - 0xd0], xmm0
0x407f62:	movaps	xmmword ptr [rbp - 0xe0], xmm0
0x407f69:	mov	qword ptr [rbp - 0xb0], 0
0x407f74:	lea	rdi, [rbp - 0xe0]
0x407f7b:	call	0x4032c0

>> _ZN3std3sys4unix6thread5guard4init17ha4e308f7b00e4ed6E
>> _ZN3std2rt19lang_start_internal17hd640de3aab7bd269E
>> main
>> __libc_start_main
>> None
```
Note that `rbp` is not 16 byte aligned, so the `movaps` is going to fail with a protection fault.

If I run the same program directly, it works, and if I break at the offending instruction in `gdb`, `rbp` is indeed 16 byte aligned.

## Configuration
```
$ uname -a
Linux sujayakar-vm 4.4.0-119-generic #143-Ubuntu SMP Mon Apr 2 16:08:24 UTC 2018 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
$ rustc --version
rustc 1.27.0-nightly (79252ff4e 2018-04-29)
```

issue_title: Converting 2.5 criteria files to 3.0 criteria files
issue_body:
It came up at the workshop that it would be handy if the PmagPy 2.5 --> 3.0 conversion could handle criteria files, so I took a stab at it.

I didn’t have all the data I need in acceptance_criteria to write a valid 3.0 criteria file.  

    {'samples.int_n_specimens': {'category': None,
      'decimal_points': -999,
      'threshold_type': 'inherited',
      'value': '3'},
     ….


So I modified pmag.read_criteria_from_file to include some additional data (‘pmag_criteria_code’, ‘er_citation_names’, and ‘criteria_definition’ if available):

    {'sample_int_n': {'category': None,
      'criteria_definition': 'acceptance criteria for study',
      'decimal_points': -999,
      'er_citation_names': 'This study',
      'pmag_criteria_code': 'ACCEPT',
      'threshold_type': 'inherited',
      'value': '3'},


Then I wrote a new function, pmag.convert_criteria_file_2_to_3, which: 
- uses the data from pmag.read_criteria_from_file
- get criterion_operation from the data model
- and assembles everything into proper 3.0 format.

Everything looked good, but then I realized that I was missing a few items.  My conversion script works properly if there is only one criterion (ACCEPT, IE-SPEC, etc), but can break with multiple criteria.

The problem is that pmag.read_criteria_from_file stores values in a dictionary, and so if a table-column is used in more than one place, the value will be overwritten.  

i.e., if a criteria file looks like this (McMurdo 2.5):

    criteria_definition	er_citation_names	pmag_criteria_code    site_polarity

    Criteria for inclusion in normal mean	This study	NPOLE	    n  
    Criteria for inclusion in reverse mean	This study	RPOLE	    r

pmag.read_criteria_from_file will munch it and end up with only one of the two rows needed for site_polarity, since you can't have a dictionary with two 'site_polarity' keys. 

I could write a second version of pmag.read_criteria_from_file that stores those data differently, but that seems a bit redundant.  I could also re-write pmag.read_criteria_from_file to store the data a little differently {"site_polarity": {}, "site_polarity2": {}}, or something like that, and then rename them at the very end.  I'm just a bit nervous about breaking criteria functionality elsewhere.  

@ltauxe, I know you are out and about but maybe we can discuss this when you get back?

issue_title: Thrift structures that are equal do not hash to the same value
issue_body:
Given the following thrift file:
```
struct Test {
    1: required string a
    2: required string b
}
```

```
Python 2.7.13 (default, Dec 18 2016, 07:03:39)
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

IPython 5.1.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
?         -> Introduction and overview of IPython's features.
%quickref -> Quick reference.
help      -> Python's own help system.
object?   -> Details about 'object', use 'object??' for extra details.

In [1]: import thriftrw

In [2]: test = thriftrw.load('./test.thrift')

In [3]: first = test.Test('a', 'b')

In [4]: second = test.Test('a', 'b')

In [5]: first == second
Out[5]: True

In [6]: hash(first) == hash(second)
Out[6]: False

In [7]: frozenset([first]) == frozenset([second])
Out[7]: False
```

I believe this means the code generation for the `Test` structure doesn't implement `hash`.

issue_title: TFlearn regression implemented incorrectly
issue_body: This relates to #343 and a few others. I am moving some code from a demo of an LSTM to tflearn: https://github.com/tgjeon/TensorFlow-Tutorials-for-Time-Series/blob/master/lstm-for-epf.py. It doesn't work with the latest Tensorflow, some of the contrib libraries are no longer available and such. I tried implement the corresponding network in tflearn and digging deeper into the network I found a bug with TFlearn regression implementation. It's not actually implemented. @aymericdamien implemented a tensorflow example of regression https://github.com/aymericdamien/TensorFlow-Examples/blob/master/examples/2_BasicModels/linear_regression.py: ` ... # Construct a linear model pred = tf.add(tf.mul(X, W), b) # Mean squared error cost = tf.reduce_sum(tf.pow(pred-Y, 2))/(2*n_samples) # Gradient descent optimizer = tf.train.GradientDescentOptimizer(learning_rate).minimize(cost) ` Well looking at the simple regression example with tflearn at https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/basics/linear_regression.py you get a completely different graph - devoid of weights, biases and formula. You get just y. Please find the pictures below - one with regression that works with tensorflow and the other with tflearn. I suspect @aymericdamien could fix this in a jiffy by putting in Just to help out, there is a function in tensorflow.python.ops import nn, that has nn.xw_plus_b(tensor_in, weights, biases) Thanks in advance, Andrew

issue_title: 'test' doesn't correctly evaluate multi-label results
issue_body:
I've got a set of news articles I'm trying to classify according to topic (eg "education","economy","crime" etc...).
I've got some sets of manually tagged data which I'm using to build a model.bin (using the 'supervised' option). Some articles cover multiple topics, and thus have multiple labels. Some articles don't cover any of the topics we're interested in, and have no labels at all.
However, the 'test' option assumes that every article should have exactly 'k' labels, regardless of how improbable those labels are scored.
So the resultant precision/recall scores can be waaaaaay out - even when testing against the same dataset used to train the model in the first place!

The only solution I've come up with is to add a probability threshold option to the 'test' operation - any labels with a probability below the threshold would be discarded. Of course then there's the problem of tuning that threshold to an appropriate level... but hey. I doubt there's a 'correct' level, so it's probably a case of leaving it up to the user to tune.

I'm happy to have a go at implementing this - it looks straightforward enough. But is it actually a sane and reasonable approach?

issue_title: std::chrono::duration<float> def_readwrite wrap does not compile.
np.bool_ is not autoconverted to C++ bool unlike other numpy numeric types
issue_body:
## Issue description

It looks like all numpy types are autoconverted to C++-level int/double/etc. as needed, *with the exception of np.bool_*.  I'd think np.bool_ should likewise get autoconverted to a C++ bool.

## Reproducible example code
Compiling the example repository pybind11/python_example with the following patch applied:
```
diff --git a/src/main.cpp b/src/main.cpp
index d81046d..8adbe25 100644
--- a/src/main.cpp
+++ b/src/main.cpp
@@ -1,6 +1,6 @@
 #include <pybind11/pybind11.h>

-int add(int i, int j) {
+int add(int i, bool j) {
     return i + j;
 }
```
Then
```
python_example.add(np.int64(1), True)
```
works, but
```
python_example.add(1, np.bool_(True))
```
fails with a TypeError.


(initially reported on gitter)

issue_title: PYBIND11_MODULE namespace clash
Static properties override
issue_body:
Hello!

The question: do static properties override allowed by pybind11?

I have a base class, say `Foo` defined with its Python bindings in `module1`. Also I have a derived class `Bar: public Foo` defined with its Python bindings in `module2` (`pybind11::class_<Bar, Foo>`, i.e. base class `Foo` is passed as template argument to `pybind11::class_`). Both have static method `int test()` which I want to export to Python as static readonly property. I accomplish this with `.def_property_readonly_static` following the documentation.

But when `module2` is being loaded in Python interpreter I'm getting an exception: `AttributeError: can't set attribute`. I can fix this if I export `test()` as static function, i.e. with `.def_static` -- then everything seems to work properly. But overriding static properties doesn't work for me.

I've read a topic about splitting the code between multiple modules. I have symbols visibility set up correctly for sure. Documentation suggests to add `py::module::import("basic")` in the beginning if binding code. But If I do this in `module2`, I face an exception: `ImportError: No module named basic`. Are the docs up to date in this section?

issue_title: Where to explain performance tips ?
issue_body:
Hello,
in our struggle for performance we've found a cheap optimization : using the C++ version of protobuf instead of the python one. It's all decribed [here](https://github.com/google/protobuf/tree/v2.6.1/python) : just set two environment variables before you run your program (PROTOCOL_BUFFERS_PYTHON_IMPLEMENTATION=cpp and ROTOCOL_BUFFERS_PYTHON_IMPLEMENTATION_VERSION=2) and you get speedup for free (25 % in our usecase without quantization nor winding order). You might ask why version 2.6.1 ? Because we're on debian 8, and we use the same python package version than the apt python-protobuf package.

So, now my question is : is there somewhere in the project where we can leave track of this tip ?

issue_title: Make sure the client crashes on every unhandled exception (new approach)
issue_body:
The raiden client is supposed to crash on every unhandled exception. However, unhandled exceptions that happen in spawned `gevent.Greenlet`s only crash their greenlet and do not bubble up on the greenlet tree by default. Therefore, every greenlet will be required to check for unhandled exceptions in its children and propagate them.

Our former approach to achieve this behavior was to patch gevent and mess with its exception handling. After several attempts and discussions involving @andrevmatos, @ulope, @hackaugusto and myself we came to the conclusion that there is no way to achieve the behavior we want by patching gevent without unmanageable side effects.

Replaces #1829 and #2123 

To do:

- [ ] Revert gevent patching
- [ ] Enforce crashing on each unhandled exception within the rest API
- [ ] keep a list of long-running greenlets and watch for exceptions in them
- [ ] avoid the spawn-and-forget pattern, rewrite functions where necessary

issue_title: Social auth pipeline customization questions -- create_user filtering
issue_body:
I am using both GoogleOAuth2 and Django backends, and need to set up create_user to only occur for particular email domains, but I am finding that the code fails to permit pre-existing accounts to log in.  That is to say, 'create_user' seems to fail for the standard django model backend -- are there hooks here that prevent pipelines from cascading to associated_user?  How does the code choose to continue on the pipeline? How can we choose to abort the pipeline?

In my example code below, users at 'trusted.com' can sign in any time.  Users that I create in django-admin are not able to log in after the code change.

I need to set up social auth to ensure that users at 'trusted.com' can sign in any time, even without an account being created in advance.  Users at 'untrusted.com' chould be able to sign in, only if an administrator creates their account in Django in advance (eg, django admin).  Unsolicited 'untrusted.com' users should not be able to sign in.

For example, Django 'users' database has these users already created:

**Existing accounts:**
user1@trusted.com
user2@untrusted.com

When user1 and user2 log in using Google Auth, I expect them to authenticate fine.

I want to trust users at 'trusted.com' to log in and have an account automatically created for them. (This works fine)

**New user, trusted domain logging in via Google Auth**
user3@trusted.com

However, I want to ensure new, unsolicited accounts cannot log in
**New user, should be denied**
user4@unwanted.com


I set up 'create_user' and opted to 'return' without any user objects if the email address is not in the trusted list - it's a blank list.

After this, users at 'trusted.com' are able to log in, but users at 'untrusted.com' are never able to log in, even if they have an account.



**settings.py:**

```python
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = (
    'social_core.backends.google.GoogleOAuth2',
    'django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend',
)
SOCIAL_AUTH_PIPELINE = (
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.social_details',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.social_uid',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.auth_allowed',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.social_user',
    'social_core.pipeline.user.get_username',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.associate_by_email', 
    'auth_pipeline.create_user',
    #'social_core.pipeline.user.create_user',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.associate_user',
    'social_core.pipeline.social_auth.load_extra_data',
    'social_core.pipeline.user.user_details',
)
```
**auth_pipeline.py:**
```python
ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS = ['trusted.com']

def allowed_email(email):
    for email_domain in ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS:
        if email_domain in email:
            return True
    return False

def create_user(strategy, details, backend, user=None, *args, **kwargs):
    """ Replaces the social.pipeline.user.create_user function for valid email check
    """

    if user:
        return {'is_new': False}
    
    if not allowed_email(details.get('email')):
        return

    fields = dict(
            (name, kwargs.get(name, details.get(name))
             )
                  for name in backend.setting('USER_FIELDS', USER_FIELDS))
    if not fields:
        return

    return {
        'is_new': True,
        'user': strategy.create_user(**fields)
    }

```

issue_title: Add an import command
issue_body:
We have `get` functions that let you read in the existing state of a resource, by its ID, without adding it to your checkpoint file. And we have a `refresh` command that will take existing resources in your checkpoint, and synchronize your checkpoint's state with the live values of the resources.

What we are missing is a way to import existing resources into your checkpoint, essentially "adopting" them for use within Pulumi.  For large existing cloud applications and infrastructure, this capability will be a requirement.

As described, the above idea is actually trivial to achieve.  Import some IDs into the checkpoint, and then refresh their state.  This is probably a fine place to start just for simplicity (it's a little better than just editing the checkpoint by hand to achieve the same thing).

One could imagine something like

```
$ pulumi import aws:elasticloadbalancingv2:Listener mylb-42dc3ff4
```

and having it result in an imported resource in your checkpoint file, with the live resource state.  Presumably running `pulumi update` after this would think the resource had been deleted from your program, until you manually added the source code.

The problem gets complicated by two matters

1. How to discover those IDs, particularly when the mapping of physical resources to logical ones in a Pulumi program are not always evident (and not always even 1-to-1)

2. How to generate the source code for the program that would have created that checkpoint state

Those feel like longer term problems that we'll need to tackle, perhaps as follow-on activities.

I should note that all these issues share a lot in common with our ongoing design discussion of conversion tools.

issue_title: Add timeout support to the test harness
An equivalent of __context__, but for exceptions that got pre-empted by another exception unwinding the stack
issue_body:
As @nikratio points out in https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/issues/30, Trio's way of propagating exceptions can lead to obscure results when you have multiple communicating tasks where a crash in one of them triggers a crash in another. Depending on timing and the presence of checkpoints in the first task's cleanup clauses, you can end up in a situation where the second exception propagates up to a common nursery, triggers a cancellation of the original task, and this cancellation wipes out the original exception, leaving you scratching your head over the root cause.

Here's a simplified version of the original example:

```python
import trio, trio.testing

async def echo_server(server_stream):
    try:
        async with server_stream:
            data = await server_stream.receive_some(10)
            await server_stream.send_lal(data)          # <--- notice the typo
    finally:
        # Pretend we had some other cleanup to do
        await trio.hazmat.checkpoint()
        await trio.hazmat.checkpoint()

async def echo_client(client_stream):
    await client_stream.send_all(b"x")
    assert await client_stream.receive_some(1) == b"x"

async def main():
    client_stream, server_stream = trio.testing.lockstep_stream_pair()
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(echo_server, server_stream)
        nursery.start_soon(echo_client, client_stream)

trio.run(main)
```

The exact details might change depending on trio version. For me right now on 0.3.0, putting one checkpoint in the `finally` block gives me a `MultiError([AssertionError, AttributeError])`, and putting two checkpoints gives me just a plain `AssertionError` – the `AttributeError` has disappeared.

There doesn't seem to be any way to actually preserve the `AttributeError` here – the `echo_server` task caught it, and then got cancelled while handling it. In this case it would eventually have propagated out, but in general there's no way to know that. Maybe it was caught for real. In @nikratio's example, it wasn't going to propagate further, but was going to get logged.

However, taking a page from Python 3's implicit exception chaining, we *can* at least preserve the information that the `AssertionError` *preempted* the `AttributeError`, so the information is available later when trying to figure out wtf happened. At least in principle.

One possible approach:

- Implement #285, so that nurseries can peek at the `Cancelled` exceptions that were used to unwind other branches of the stack

- if any of these `Cancelled` exceptions have `__context__` values, gather those up

- attach them to the exception that the nursery re-raises, in a new `__preempted__` attribute or similar (it's tempting to wedge this into `__context__` instead of making something new, but I don't think we can really do that meaningfully)

- update our traceback printing code to check for `__preempted__`, and say something about it

One trick is how to record `__preempted__`, given that we can have complicated situations like: the same exception passing upwards through multiple nurseries, and preempting some exceptions at each one. Or, a `MultiError` that pre-empts some other exceptions, but then part of the `MultiError` gets caught and it converts back into a regular single exception.

Idea: make `__preempted__` a dict mapping frames to sets of preempted exceptions – with the idea that the frame records *where* during the unwinding the preemption took place. When we filter a `MultiError`, preserve and combine the `__preempted__` from `MultiError` objects that get collapsed. When printing, make a note at the point in the stack where the preemption happened. Maybe the default is that we print a little note like "(at this point, preempted: RuntimeError, ValueError)" and give an envvar that can be set to get full details?

Regarding #285, it might make sense to apply this logic to `TooSlowError`s too... maybe that'd just be clutter though, dunno.

@pk-g 
I am working on LSTM.
Current test cases does not have cases about input's seq_len != 1.
I am curious how it works. 
I think it should like following. But the output mismatches with output from pytorch. (I set same input, w, r, b)
Could you take a look? 
```
    def step(self):
        [w_i, w_o, w_f, w_c] = np.split(self.W, 4)
        [r_i, r_o, r_f, r_c] = np.split(self.R, 4)
        [p_i, p_o, p_f] = np.split(self.P, 3)
        [w_bi, w_bo, w_bf, w_bc, r_bi, r_bo, r_bf, r_bc] = np.split(self.B, 8)
        h_list = []
        for x in np.split(self.X, self.X.shape[0], axis=0):
            i = self.f(np.dot(x, np.transpose(w_i)) + np.dot(self.H_0, r_i) + w_bi + r_bi + p_i * self.C_0)
            f = self.f(np.dot(x, np.transpose(w_f)) + np.dot(self.H_0, r_f) + w_bf + r_bf + p_f * self.C_0)
            c = self.g(np.dot(x, np.transpose(w_c)) + np.dot(self.H_0, r_c) + w_bc + r_bc)
            C = f * self.C_0 + i * c
            o = self.f(np.dot(x, np.transpose(w_o)) + np.dot(self.H_0, r_o) + w_bo + r_bo + p_o * C)
            H = o * self.h(C)
            h_list.append(H)
            self.H_0 = H
            self.C_0 = C
        return np.concatenate(h_list)
```

issue_title: Various problems with Conv2D and Conv2DLayer.
issue_body:
### New Issue Checklist

- [x] I have read the [Contribution Guidelines](https://github.com/tensorlayer/tensorlayer/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [x] I searched for [existing GitHub issues](https://github.com/tensorlayer/tensorlayer/issues)

### Issue Description

These two layers breaks when using "NCHW" format. There is a related issue #297.

In `Conv2dLayer`, you have

```python
class Conv2dLayer(Layer):
    # breaks when using "NCHW" format, should use tf.nn.bias_add instead
    self.outputs = act(tf.nn.conv2d(...) + b)
```

Also in the `Conv2D` wrapper, the dimension order of `strides` is wrong when using the "NCHW" format. See [tf.nn.conv2d](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/nn/conv2d).

Plus, the documentation about the `strides` parameter of these two layers are also wrong.

issue_title: Spell-Correct: Probability of all corrected words are the same
issue_body:
In pythainlp/pythainlp/spell/pn.py, you said you fork the code from http://norvig.com/spell-correct.html. As far as I understand, you use the same implementation as in the link. 

In your code, you import "WORDS" from **dictionary**. Instead the link above use **corpus** (big.txt) rather than dictionary. This make the probability of the corrected words are the same because all words appear only once. The idea behind this code is to chose the most frequent word in the corpus. 

Just change "WORDS" to the big corpus.

issue_title: Object Interface
issue_body:
azarot...@gmail.com, 2009-07-15T17:44:26.000Z 
Attached object oriented interface for non RDF aware developers to build
RDF graphs for consideration.

Comment 1 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 2 by gromgull
Is this not quite similar to Sparta? The "resource centric" API on top of rdflib?

http://github.com/mnot/sparta/


Comment 3 by drewpca
I think a module like this does not belong in core rdflib, but I would encourage you 
to continue to develop it separately. (And have a look at sparta :)

I recommend WontFix


Comment 4 by eik...@gmail.com
Perhaps at some future date we can revisit adding such an interface to rdflib, but for now I agree with drew that 
this is best developed separately, at least for now.


Comment 5 by lindstr...@gmail.com
Agreed. Apart from this code and Sparta, there's also Oort, RdfAlchemy and SuRF 
providing similar object mappers for RDF (using RDFLib).
